← World Gallery
Regions Artists Timeline All works

Timeline

All 719 works in chronological order — cross-cultural contemporaries lined up.
Click any image to enlarge. Use ← → or close with Esc.

Before 1000 BCE108 works

75000 BCE
Africa
Blombos Cave Engravings
Middle Stone Age · c. 75,000 BCE · South Africa
Deliberate crosshatch patterns incised on ochre and shell beads perforated with bone awls — among the earliest evidence of symbolic thought ever found. The cave pushed back the dawn of human art by tens of thousands of years.
30000 BCE
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Uluru
Aṉangu · sacred site · Australia
A sandstone monolith rising 1,100 feet out of the center of the continent. For the Aṉangu it is the physical record of ancestral beings' creation journeys.
15000 BCE
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Indigenous Australian Art
Aboriginal · millennia to present · Australia
Bradshaw paintings (Gwion Gwion) in the Kimberley, possibly 20,000 years old — elongated figures painted with pigment that has bonded permanently with the rock surface.
10000 BCE
Africa
Tsodilo Hills Rock Paintings
San · c. 24,000 BCE–1800 CE · Botswana
More than four thousand paintings on the quartzite outcrops the San call 'the Louvre of the desert.' Eland and giraffes predominate, but red-pigment handprints dating to the last Ice Age make this one of the longest continuously used sacred sites on earth.
4000 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Tell Brak
Uruk-period urban center · c. 4000 BCE · northeast Syria
One of the world's earliest cities, older than Uruk itself, dominated by the Eye Temple and thousands of small alabaster 'eye idols' — figurines reduced to enormous staring pupils. A mass grave nearby suggests urban life here could end violently.
4000 BCE
Americas
Cueva de las Manos
Patagonian hunter-gatherers · 7300–700 BCE · Argentina
A cave with hundreds of human hand stencils, mostly left hands — because the artists were holding the bone tube of pigment in the right, blowing around it.
3500 BCE
Africa
Laas Geel Rock Paintings
Neolithic pastoralist · c. 3500 BCE · Somaliland
A complex of granite shelters filled with some of Africa's best-preserved rock art: cattle draped in ceremonial robes, their horns painted in alternating red and white, with small human figures raising arms beneath them. Only rediscovered by archaeologists in 2002.
3500 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Mesopotamian Cylinder Seals
Sumerian onward · 4th millennium BCE onward
Tiny carved stone cylinders rolled across clay to sign a document. Each person owned one — an identity card and an art form at once.
3200 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Warka Vase
Uruk period · c. 3200 BCE · Sumer
One of the earliest known narrative reliefs — a procession to the goddess Inanna. Looted from the Iraq Museum in 2003 and later recovered, broken.
3200 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Kish Tablet
Early Sumerian · c. 3200 BCE
A small limestone tablet from Kish covered in proto-cuneiform signs — possibly the earliest known writing. Accounts of barley, fish, and a threshing sled.
3100 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Narmer Palette
Early Dynastic · c. 3100 BCE
A ceremonial siltstone palette showing a king smiting an enemy — one of the earliest monuments of unified Egypt, with its conventions already fully formed.
3100 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Memphis (Egypt)
Early Dynastic onward · c. 3100 BCE · Lower Egypt
The first capital of unified Egypt, sacred to Ptah. What survives today is ruins in a palm grove — a colossal reclining statue of Ramesses II, sphinxes, embalmed Apis bulls.
3100 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Mask of Warka
Uruk · c. 3100 BCE · Sumer
The first known naturalistic human face in art — a white marble mask, probably of the goddess Inanna. Looted in 2003, recovered in a farmer's garden.
3000 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Stele of King Djet (The Serpent King)
c. 3000 BCE · Limestone · Sully Wing
Over 5,000 years old — from the very dawn of writing. A serpent (the king's name) stands above the serekh, the palace façade, topped by Horus the falcon. This is what Egyptian writing looked like before it became hieroglyphs.
3000 BCE
East Asia
Jōmon Flame Pottery
Jōmon · c. 3000 BCE · Japan
Clay pots with rims twisted into exuberant "flame" shapes. Made by prehistoric hunter-gatherers — and, at 15,000 years old, among the oldest pottery traditions on earth.
2700 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Tell Asmar Hoard
Early Dynastic Sumerian · c. 2700 BCE · Eshnunna
A cache of twelve alabaster worshiper statues with oversized inlaid eyes of shell and bitumen, designed to gaze eternally at the deity. Found buried beneath the floor of the Square Temple, they stood in for their absent owners in perpetual prayer.
2670 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Pyramid of Djoser
Old Kingdom · c. 2670 BCE · Saqqara
The first pyramid in Egypt — six stacked mastabas designed by the architect Imhotep, later deified as a god of wisdom and medicine.
2600 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Pyramid of Meidum
Old Kingdom · c. 2600 BCE
A failed smooth-sided pyramid whose outer casing collapsed. What survives is the blunt tower at the core — an architectural experiment that taught the builders of Giza.
2600 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Bent Pyramid
Old Kingdom · Sneferu · c. 2600 BCE · Dahshur
Sneferu's second pyramid — the angle changes partway up, either to prevent collapse or because ambition had to meet physics. A crucial step between stepped and smooth pyramids.
2600 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Standard of Ur
Sumerian · c. 2600 BCE
A wooden box inlaid with lapis, shell, and red limestone. One side shows war; the other, a banquet after the victory.
2600 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Queen Puabi's Headdress
Sumerian · c. 2600 BCE · Ur
Gold leaves, lapis lazuli pendants, carnelian beads, tied with ribbon hair-wire. Queen Puabi went to her grave wearing this, attended by 23 servants — also buried.
2600 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Royal Cemetery of Ur
Sumerian · c. 2600 BCE · Iraq
Sixteen royal graves, each with servants, soldiers, and musicians sacrificed and buried beside the king. Out came the Standard of Ur, Queen Puabi's headdress, and the Ram in the Thicket.
2600 BCE
Americas
Caral
Norte Chico · c. 2600 BCE · Peru
A city of six pyramids and sunken plazas older than the pyramids of Giza. Without pottery, ceramics, or writing, its builders sustained a monumental civilization on irrigation and anchovy fishing from Peru's central coast.
2575 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Red Pyramid
Old Kingdom · Sneferu · c. 2575 BCE · Dahshur
Sneferu's third pyramid — the first true smooth-sided pyramid to work. Named for the reddish limestone exposed after the original white casing was stripped for later buildings.
2570 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Khafre Enthroned
Old Kingdom · c. 2570 BCE · Giza
Diorite portrait of the pharaoh who built the second pyramid. Horus the falcon wraps his wings around the back of the king's head — divine kingship locked in stone.
2570 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Seated Statue of Hemiunu
Dynasty IV · c. 2570 BCE · Giza
A life-size limestone portrait of the vizier who is thought to have overseen construction of Khufu's Great Pyramid. His ample stomach and heavy jowls were a deliberate display of elite wealth — the opposite of the idealized kingly physique.
2560 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Great Pyramid of Giza
Old Kingdom · c. 2560 BCE
Tomb of Khufu. 2.3 million stone blocks, averaging 2.5 tons each. The tallest human-made structure on earth for 3,800 years.
2560 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
The Seated Scribe
Old Kingdom · c. 2620–2500 BCE · Saqqara
Painted limestone, eyes inlaid with crystal and copper. Alert and ready, reed in hand — the realist portrait of a bureaucrat, 4,500 years ago.
2550 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Reserve Heads
Dynasty IV · c. 2550 BCE · Giza
Limestone heads placed in Old Kingdom tombs as substitute bodies in case the mummy was destroyed. Their ears are deliberately chipped off or their necks grooved — probably a ritual 'killing' to prevent the spirit from wandering back into the land of the living.
2500 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Great Sphinx of Giza
Old Kingdom · c. 2500 BCE
Carved from a single limestone outcrop, the oldest known monumental sculpture. Its nose was already missing before Napoleon arrived.
2500 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Ram in a Thicket
Sumerian · c. 2500 BCE · Royal Cemetery of Ur
A goat rearing against a golden bush, made of wood, gold leaf, silver, lapis, and shell. Leonard Woolley named it for Abraham's offering in Genesis.
2500 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Lyres of Ur
Sumerian · c. 2500 BCE · Royal Cemetery
Wooden lyres covered in gold and lapis, the sound boxes shaped into bulls' heads. Found in Queen Puabi's grave next to the women who played them. The oldest stringed instruments we have.
2500 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Sumerian Libation Plaque
Early Dynastic · c. 2500 BCE · Ur
A perforated stone plaque once hung in a temple. The top register shows a naked priest pouring an offering to a seated god; the lower register, worshipers bringing livestock.
2500 BCE
South Asia
Mohenjo-daro
Indus Valley Civilization · c. 2500 BCE · Pakistan
A Bronze Age city with grid streets, covered drains, and a Great Bath. Its script is still undeciphered.
2500 BCE
South Asia
Harappa
Indus Valley Civilization · c. 2600–1900 BCE · Pakistan
One of the two great Indus cities, grid-planned like Mohenjo-daro. Its granary, citadel, and burial mounds lie under centuries of British railway ballast quarried from the ruins.
2450 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Stele of the Vultures
Sumerian · c. 2450 BCE · Lagash
The earliest known battle monument. King Eannatum's army marches in lockstep behind their shields; on the other side, vultures carry off the heads of the defeated.
2400 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Mastaba of Ti
Old Kingdom · c. 2400 BCE · Saqqara
The tomb of a minor royal official, painted inside with daily-life scenes — farming, fishing, ships under sail — and regarded as the high point of Old Kingdom relief.
2400 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Palermo Stone
Old Kingdom royal annals · c. 2400 BCE · Egypt
A black basalt slab inscribed with the reigns, heights of Nile floods, and major rituals of every king from the predynastic era through Dynasty V. Only seven fragments survive of what must have been a two-meter-tall master chronology.
2400 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Pyramid Texts
Old Kingdom · c. 2400 BCE · Saqqara
The oldest religious corpus on earth, carved into the walls of royal burial chambers at Saqqara. Written in Old Egyptian and painted turquoise, the spells teach the dead king how to fly to the sky, evade demons, and rejoin his father the sun.
2400 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Statue of Ebih-Il
Sumerian · c. 2400 BCE · Mari
An administrator from Mari, seated with hands folded in prayer, dressed in a tufted skirt. Lapis and shell eyes still stare from a white gypsum face.
2350 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Ebla Tablets
Early Bronze Age archive · c. 2350 BCE · Syria
About 17,000 cuneiform tablets found stacked on collapsed wooden shelves in the burned royal palace of Tell Mardikh. Written in an early Semitic language previously unknown, they revealed that Syria was already a literate, cosmopolitan kingdom a thousand years before Hammurabi.
2345 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Pyramid of Unas
Dynasty V · c. 2345 BCE · Saqqara
A modest pyramid whose burial chamber is inscribed from floor to ceiling with the Pyramid Texts — the earliest religious writings known. Blue-painted hieroglyphs cover 283 spells guiding the king's ascent to the imperishable stars.
2330 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Victory Stele of Sargon of Akkad
Akkadian · c. 2330 BCE · Iraq (found at Susa)
A heavily damaged limestone stele showing Sargon, the world's first empire-builder, leading bound captives while vultures pick at the fallen. Its hierarchical scaling — king monumental, enemies tiny — fixed a template that would outlast Mesopotamia by millennia.
2300 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Ebla
Early Bronze Age · c. 2500–1600 BCE · Syria
A Syrian city-state whose palace archive held 20,000 cuneiform tablets in a previously unknown Semitic language. Discovered in the 1970s — changed the textbook on the Bronze Age Levant.
2300 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Anubanini Rock Relief
Lullubi kingdom · c. 2300 BCE · Kermanshah, Iran
The victorious king Anubanini tramples a prostrate foe while the goddess Ishtar delivers captives on a leash. Carved into a vertical cliff, it inspired Darius's later Behistun monument four kilometers away.
2250 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Victory Stele of Naram-Sin
Akkadian · c. 2250 BCE
The king climbs a mountain over dying enemies, horned helmet marking him as divine. The first ruler in history to have himself depicted as a god.
2200 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Great Sphinx of Tanis
Old to Middle Kingdom · c. 2600–1800 BCE
One of the largest sphinxes outside Giza — reused and re-inscribed by pharaoh after pharaoh. Now guards the entrance of the Louvre's Egyptian galleries.
2200 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Mari Ziggurat
Early Dynastic → Old Babylonian · c. 2500–1750 BCE · Syria
A trading capital on the middle Euphrates. Its palace mural of a royal investiture is one of the oldest paintings on plaster to survive.
2175 BCE
South Asia
Pashupati Seal
Indus Valley · c. 2350–2000 BCE
A steatite seal of a seated, horned figure surrounded by animals — sometimes read as a proto-Shiva, sometimes a shaman. We can't read the script that runs along the top.
2120 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Statue of Gudea
Neo-Sumerian · c. 2120 BCE · Lagash
More than twenty small diorite statues of the prince of Lagash survive, each showing him at prayer. The diorite was hauled from the Sinai; carving it took tools that barely existed.
2100 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Ziggurat of Ur
Neo-Sumerian · c. 2100 BCE · Iraq
A stepped temple-platform to the moon god Nanna. Its baked-brick facade has been partly restored; the core is as old as the pyramids.
2100 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Code of Ur-Nammu
Neo-Sumerian · c. 2100 BCE
The oldest known written law code, predating Hammurabi by three centuries. The laws are oddly lenient — mostly fines, only occasional death.
2100 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Eridu Ziggurat
Ubaid–Ur III · c. 5400–2100 BCE · southern Iraq
Thought to be the oldest city in Mesopotamia. Eighteen superimposed temple layers stand beneath the surviving ziggurat of Ur-Nammu, built over a succession of shrines to the water god Enki stretching back five thousand years.
2030 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Statue of Mentuhotep II
Dynasty XI · c. 2030 BCE · Deir el-Bahari
The unifier of Egypt after the First Intermediate Period, painted with the black skin of Osiris and wearing the red crown of Lower Egypt. His deliberately massive, almost archaic body announces a new Middle Kingdom style grounded in reborn Upper Egyptian traditions.
2000 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Karnak Temple Complex
New Kingdom onward · 2000 BCE – 100 CE
A city of temples at Thebes, built and rebuilt by thirty dynasties. The hypostyle hall alone has 134 columns.
2000 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Kerma
Kerma culture · c. 2500–1500 BCE · Sudan
Capital of the first kingdom of Nubia, whose massive mud-brick Western Deffufa still rises 18 meters. The royal tumuli concealed astonishing human sacrifices — hundreds of courtiers buried alive around their king to serve him in the afterlife.
2000 BCE
South Asia
Priest-King of Mohenjo-daro
Indus Valley · c. 2000 BCE · Pakistan
A small bearded steatite bust, head-band and trefoil-patterned robe. Its title is a guess: we don't know if Mohenjo-daro had priests, kings, or this man at all.
1981 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Models from the Tomb of Meketre
Dynasty XI · c. 1981 BCE · Thebes
Dozens of painted wooden miniatures — a cattle count, weaving workshops, porticoed villas, fishing boats — sealed in a hidden chamber beside Meketre's tomb. Discovered intact in 1920, they provide the most detailed snapshot of Middle Kingdom daily life.
1950 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
White Chapel of Senusret I
Middle Kingdom · c. 1950 BCE · Karnak
A small bark chapel of fine white limestone. Torn down as filler in a later pylon; found reassembled piece by piece and reconstructed in the 1930s.
1900 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Prisse Papyrus
Middle Kingdom · c. 1900 BCE
The oldest literary book in existence. Two teachings of wisdom — "the Instructions of Kagemni" and "of Ptahhotep" — carefully written in cursive hieroglyphs.
1900 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Beni Hasan
Middle Kingdom · c. 1900 BCE
Thirty-nine rock-cut tombs of provincial governors, walls painted with daily life — wrestling matches, bakers, looms, dancers — a rare survival of Middle Kingdom domestic art.
1900 BCE
Mediterranean
Palace of Knossos
Minoan · c. 1900 BCE · Crete
A sprawling Bronze Age palace with running water, frescoes of dolphins and bull-leapers, and a storage maze that may be the origin of the Minotaur myth.
1800 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Burney Relief
Old Babylonian · c. 1800 BCE
A nude winged goddess standing on two lions, flanked by owls. Most likely Ereshkigal, queen of the underworld — or Inanna, descending into it.
1800 BCE
Mediterranean
Kamares Ware
Middle Minoan · c. 1800 BCE · Crete
Thin-walled polychrome pottery painted with rippling tendrils and marine forms on a glossy black ground. Named after the sacred cave on Mount Ida where the first examples were found tossed as offerings.
1754 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Code of Hammurabi
Old Babylonian · c. 1754 BCE
Diorite stele, seven feet tall. 282 laws in cuneiform — "an eye for an eye" comes from here. Now in the Louvre.
1750 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Babylon
Old Babylonian → Neo-Babylonian · 18th c. BCE – 6th c. BCE
Capital of Hammurabi, Nebuchadnezzar, and Cyrus. Reconstructed in the 1980s by Saddam Hussein with bricks stamped in his own name.
1700 BCE
Mediterranean
Phaistos Disc
Minoan · c. 1700 BCE · Crete
A fired clay disk stamped with 241 signs in a spiral. Still undeciphered. One of the earliest known uses of movable type — by more than 2,500 years.
1600 BCE
Mediterranean
Snake Goddess
Minoan faience · c. 1600 BCE · Knossos
A bare-breasted figurine gripping writhing snakes, her flounced skirt falling in tight registers, a feline perched on her head. Found in the palace 'Temple Repository' pits at Knossos, she is probably a household deity or priestess rather than a goddess.
1600 BCE
Mediterranean
Akrotiri Frescoes
Cycladic/Minoan · c. 1600 BCE · Thera (Santorini)
A Bronze Age town buried by the Thera eruption in meters of ash — its plastered rooms preserved images of saffron-picking girls, fishermen, and a miniature harbor fleet. When excavation began in 1967 the colors were still bright.
1600 BCE
Mediterranean
Prince of the Lilies
Minoan · c. 1600 BCE · Knossos
A reconstructed relief fresco of a figure crowned with peacock-feathered tiara striding through a field of lilies. Arthur Evans combined three scattered plaster fragments into his imagined 'Priest-King' — later scholars think the pieces likely belong to different figures entirely.
1550 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Book of the Dead
New Kingdom onward · 1550 BCE – 50 BCE
Spells written on papyrus to guide the deceased through the Duat. The Weighing of the Heart against a feather is its most famous scene.
1550 BCE
Mediterranean
Mask of Agamemnon
Mycenaean · c. 1550 BCE
A beaten-gold funerary mask from Grave Circle A at Mycenae. Schliemann announced he had "looked upon the face of Agamemnon" — though this mask is 300 years too early.
1500 BCE
Mediterranean
Bull-Leaping Fresco
Minoan · c. 1500 BCE · Knossos
Three figures vault over a charging bull on the upper east wing of Knossos: the dark-skinned leaper mid-somersault, pale women stationed before and behind. Whether ritual, sport, or myth, it remains the iconic image of Minoan Crete.
1470 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Temple of Hatshepsut
New Kingdom · c. 1470 BCE · Deir el-Bahari
Three stacked colonnaded terraces built into a cliff for Egypt's longest-reigning female pharaoh. Her successor tried to erase her — and failed.
1450 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Temple of Amun at Jebel Barkal
Kushite · c. 1450 BCE onward · Sudan
Built by Thutmose III below a sandstone butte the Nubians believed Amun lived inside. Reused by Kushite pharaohs who conquered Egypt from here in the 25th Dynasty.
1450 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Gebel Barkal
Kushite sacred mountain · c. 1450 BCE onward · Sudan
A 98-meter sandstone butte the Kushites believed housed the creator-god Amun. The New Kingdom pharaohs built a temple at its base and the Napatan kings inscribed a pinnacle-shaped outcrop with gilded reliefs now almost vanished to weather.
1450 BCE
Mediterranean
Obelisk of Theodosius
Egyptian carved 15th c. BCE, re-erected 390 CE · Istanbul
An Egyptian obelisk Thutmose III set up at Karnak; Theodosius hauled it to Constantinople and planted it on a marble base carved with Byzantine court scenes. Two monuments in one.
1400 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Deir el-Medina
New Kingdom · c. 1550–1080 BCE
The workers' village for those who built the royal tombs. Its own tombs are painted with a precision surprising for common craftsmen — because the craftsmen were the painters of the Valley of the Kings.
1400 BCE
Mediterranean
Hagia Triada Sarcophagus
Late Minoan · c. 1400 BCE · Crete
A painted limestone sarcophagus showing funeral rites on all four sides — a bull sacrifice, women carrying pails of blood, the deceased receiving offerings. Our clearest window onto Minoan ritual.
1380 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Avenue of Sphinxes
New Kingdom–Ptolemaic · c. 1380 BCE onward · Luxor
A 2.7-kilometer processional road flanked by more than 1,300 ram-headed and human-headed sphinxes connecting Luxor Temple to Karnak. Reopened in 2021 after twenty years of excavation stripped away centuries of silt and medieval housing.
1350 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Colossi of Memnon
New Kingdom · c. 1350 BCE · Luxor
Twin seated statues of Amenhotep III, sixty feet tall. After an earthquake cracked one, it "sang" at dawn for two centuries — until Romans repaired it.
1350 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Colossal Statue of Akhenaten
Amarna period · c. 1350 BCE
The heretic pharaoh depicted with a long face, wide hips, and swollen belly — a deliberate break from centuries of idealized royal portraiture.
1350 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Amarna Letters
New Kingdom · 14th c. BCE
Clay tablets of diplomatic correspondence between Egypt and its neighbors — Babylonia, Assyria, the Hittites, Canaanite city-states. Written in Akkadian, the lingua franca of the Bronze Age.
1350 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Tomb-Chapel of Nebamun
Dynasty XVIII · c. 1350 BCE · Thebes
Painted fragments from a scribe's vanished tomb, including the famous scene of Nebamun fowling in a marsh with a spotted cat retrieving birds. The butterflies in flight are rendered with a freedom the Egyptian grid rarely allowed.
1345 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Bust of Nefertiti
Amarna period · c. 1345 BCE
Painted limestone, found in the workshop of the sculptor Thutmose at Amarna. Still in Berlin; Egypt has asked for it back for a century.
1325 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Luxor Temple
New Kingdom · Amenhotep III / Ramesses II · c. 1400–1250 BCE
Every year during the Opet Festival, statues of the gods were carried in boats from Karnak to here. A Roman camp, Christian church, and mosque all followed.
1323 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Mask of Tutankhamun
New Kingdom · c. 1323 BCE
Eleven kilograms of solid gold, inlaid with lapis, carnelian, and obsidian. The funerary mask of a boy-king who died at nineteen.
1323 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Tomb of Tutankhamun
18th Dynasty · c. 1323 BCE · Valley of the Kings
A small, hastily decorated tomb, overlooked by thieves for three thousand years. Howard Carter opened it in 1922 and the world went Egyptomaniac again.
1323 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Tomb of Tutankhamun Interior (KV62)
Dynasty XVIII · c. 1323 BCE · Valley of the Kings
Yellow walls densely painted with the funerary Opening of the Mouth rite and Tutankhamun being greeted by Osiris. Oddly cramped for a king, the tomb was almost certainly borrowed after his unexpected death at about nineteen.
1300 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Valley of the Kings
New Kingdom · c. 1550–1070 BCE · West Bank, Thebes
Sixty-five painted tombs cut into the limestone valley. The pharaohs chose it for its isolation — looters found everything anyway, except Tutankhamun.
1300 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Serapeum of Saqqara
New Kingdom to Ptolemaic · c. 1300 BCE onward · Saqqara
Underground vaults housing the mummies of the sacred Apis bulls, each sealed in a granite sarcophagus weighing up to seventy tonnes. How the Egyptians maneuvered them down the narrow subterranean corridors is still debated.
1290 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Reliefs from the Tomb of Seti I
c. 1290 BCE · Painted limestone · Sully Wing
The goddess Hathor welcomes Seti I into the afterlife. Cut from the walls of KV17 in the Valley of the Kings in the 19th century — a practice we now consider vandalism, but which saved this relief from the flooding that ravaged the tomb.
1290 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Temple of Seti I at Abydos
New Kingdom · c. 1290 BCE
An L-shaped temple with seven chapels. Its Abydos King List carved on one corridor became a key scholarly tool — naming 76 pharaohs back to the First Dynasty.
1280 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Great Hypostyle Hall of Karnak
Dynasty XIX · c. 1280 BCE · Karnak
A forest of 134 lotus-bud and papyrus-bloom columns covering half a hectare, the tallest reaching 21 meters. Seti I and Ramesses II finished its decoration; inside, the sandstone sky was painted a dense blue and dotted with yellow stars.
1279 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Tomb of Seti I
19th Dynasty · c. 1279 BCE · Valley of the Kings
The longest, deepest, and most richly painted tomb in the valley. Every wall is covered in funerary texts; ceilings map the stars.
1264 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Abu Simbel
Reign of Ramesses II · c. 1264 BCE
Four colossal statues guard the temple front. In the 1960s the whole complex was cut apart and moved 200 feet up to escape Lake Nasser.
1255 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Tomb of Nefertari
19th dynasty · c. 1255 BCE · Valley of the Queens
Painted for Ramesses II's favorite wife — the queen walks through the underworld, plays the board game Senet, and greets the gods. Colors still vivid.
1250 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Ramesseum
New Kingdom · Ramesses II · 13th c. BCE · Thebes
Memorial temple of Ramesses II. A collapsed colossus here inspired Shelley's "Ozymandias" — though Shelley never saw it.
1250 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Younger Memnon (Ramesses II)
New Kingdom · c. 1250 BCE
A granite colossus of Ramesses II. Belzoni dragged it out of the Ramesseum in 1816 and shipped it to the British Museum. Shelley saw it — and wrote Ozymandias.
1250 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Colossal Statue of Ramesses II
New Kingdom · c. 1250 BCE · Grand Egyptian Museum
Eleven meters of carved granite, 83 tons. Moved from downtown Cairo to the Grand Egyptian Museum in a 2006 road spectacle so large they had to raise bridges.
1250 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Chogha Zanbil
Elamite · c. 1250 BCE · Iran
The best-preserved ziggurat in the world — five stepped stages of mud brick, built for the god Inshushinak and abandoned when the city fell.
1250 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Yazılıkaya
Hittite · 13th c. BCE · Turkey
An open-air rock sanctuary near Hattusa. Sixty-six gods and goddesses walk in procession along the cliff face — the full Hittite pantheon gathered in stone.
1250 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Hittite Seated Deity
Hittite Empire · 13th c. BCE · Anatolia
A cast bronze figure in a high conical headdress. The lost Hittite pantheon ran to thousands of names — only a handful survive in objects like this one.
1250 BCE
Mediterranean
Treasury of Atreus
Mycenaean · c. 1250 BCE · Mycenae
A tholos tomb — a stone beehive thirteen meters high, held up only by its own corbelled weight. For fifteen centuries, the largest dome in the world.
1208 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Merneptah Stele
New Kingdom · 1208 BCE
Black granite stele listing the pharaoh's victories. Line 27 contains the earliest known mention of "Israel" in any historical record.
1200 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Marlik Gold
Late Bronze Age · c. 1200 BCE · Gilan, Iran
A cemetery of about fifty-three princely tombs preserved under mudslide, yielding gold beakers embossed with winged bulls, mythical griffons, and hunting scenes. The humped-bull vessels testify to a local art tradition between the Iranian plateau and the Caspian.
1200 BCE
East Asia
Sanxingdui Bronze Masks
Sanxingdui culture · c. 1200 BCE · Sichuan
Hollow bronze and gold masks with protruding eyes and ears that fan outward. An entirely unknown civilization contemporary with Shang, found in two sacrificial pits in 1986.
1150 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Medinet Habu
New Kingdom · Ramesses III · c. 1150 BCE · Thebes
The fortress-temple of the last great warrior pharaoh. Its walls narrate the repulse of the Sea Peoples — history preserved because the victor carved it.

1000 BCE – 0102 works

1000 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Luristan Bronzes
Iron Age nomadic · c. 1200–700 BCE · Zagros Mountains, Iran
Thousands of cast-bronze horse bits, standard finials, and pins recovered from shaft graves in western Iran. Animals interlock with humans in tight symmetries that may record forgotten myths of horse-riding pastoralists.
900 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Tell Halaf
Early Iron Age · 10th c. BCE · Syria
Aramean palace built of basalt orthostats — grim, angular statues of gods, scorpions, and lions. Bombed to rubble in Berlin in 1943; a team spent decades reassembling 27,000 fragments.
900 BCE
East Asia
Chinese Ritual Bronzes
Shang–Zhou · 16th–3rd c. BCE
Tripod cauldrons, wine vessels, and axes cast for ancestor worship. Each is wrapped in a monster mask (taotie) staring from every surface.
879 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Nimrud
Neo-Assyrian · 879 BCE onward · Iraq
Ashurnasirpal II's Northwest Palace, once lined with alabaster reliefs of hunt and war. ISIS dynamited the site in 2015; much had already been moved to museums.
870 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Assyrian Winged Genii
Neo-Assyrian · c. 870 BCE · Nimrud
Four-winged protective spirits carved in alabaster lining Ashurnasirpal II's palace. They lift a pine cone and a bucket — a gesture of ritual purification never fully decoded.
865 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Relief of Ashurnasirpal II with Attendant
Neo-Assyrian · c. 865 BCE · Nimrud
Alabaster wall panels from the throne room of Ashurnasirpal's northwest palace, showing the king under a divine winged sun while an attendant purifies him with a fir cone. The minutely patterned beards and tasseled robes remained the template for Assyrian royal art for two centuries.
825 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Black Obelisk of Shalmaneser III
Neo-Assyrian · c. 825 BCE
A tribute list in stone — foreign kings bringing gifts to Shalmaneser. One is Jehu of Israel, bowing to kiss the ground. The earliest known image of an Israelite.
800 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Kul-e Farah Reliefs
Neo-Elamite · c. 1000–650 BCE · Izeh, Iran
Six rock-cut panels deep in a southwestern Iranian gorge showing Elamite rulers and banqueters in long processional sacrifices. Little known outside specialist circles, they are the best surviving witness to the visual culture of Elam after the Middle Elamite collapse.
800 BCE
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Manunggul Jar
Late Neolithic · c. 890–710 BCE · Palawan, Philippines
A secondary burial jar whose lid is crowned by two figures in a soul boat: one dead, one paddling, arms crossed, eyes closed. Among the most important prehistoric objects in Southeast Asia, it appears on the Philippine 1,000-peso note.
800 BCE
Americas
Olmec Colossal Heads
Olmec · 1200–400 BCE · Gulf coast, Mexico
Seventeen basalt heads, up to ten feet tall, each with a distinct face. Carved with stone tools and floated on rafts from the quarry, 60 miles away.
750 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Pyramids of El-Kurru
Napatan Kush · c. 860–600 BCE · Sudan
The earliest royal cemetery of the Kushite 25th Dynasty, where the pharaohs who briefly ruled Egypt lie beneath small, steep-sided pyramids. Inside, painted chambers fuse Egyptian afterlife iconography with distinctly African imagery of hunting and cattle.
750 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Lamassu
Neo-Assyrian · 9th–7th c. BCE
Winged bulls with human heads, guarding palace gates at Nineveh and Nimrud. Carved with five legs so they look correct from front and side.
750 BCE
Mediterranean
Dipylon Amphora
Geometric Greek · c. 750 BCE
A grave marker nearly five feet tall, painted with stylized mourners in a prothesis scene. The moment figurative imagery returns to Greek pottery after five centuries of pure geometry.
710 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Dur-Sharrukin (Khorsabad)
Neo-Assyrian · Sargon II · 713–706 BCE
The capital Sargon II built from scratch and moved into — only to be killed on campaign and abandoned by his heir. Its 40-ton alabaster lamassus now guard the Louvre and the Oriental Institute.
709 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Lamassu of Khorsabad
c. 713–706 BCE · Gypsum alabaster · Richelieu Wing
Human-headed winged bulls, each over 4 meters tall. They guarded the gates of Sargon II's palace in what is now northern Iraq. Carved with five legs so that they appear to be standing still when seen from the front, and striding forward when seen from the side.
700 BCE
Africa
Yeha Great Temple
D'mt kingdom · c. 700 BCE · Ethiopia
A rectangular limestone sanctuary built without mortar, oriented to the rising of the moon god Almaqah. Its precise dry-stone masonry is the oldest standing building in Ethiopia and predates the Aksumite kings by nearly a millennium.
700 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Nineveh
Neo-Assyrian capital · 7th century BCE · northern Iraq
Sennacherib's 'Palace Without Rival' and Ashurbanipal's library stood within twelve kilometers of walls. The Mashki Gate was intentionally dynamited by ISIS in 2016 and has since been partially reconstructed with its original stones.
700 BCE
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Đông Sơn Culture
Bronze Age · 700 BCE – 100 CE · Vietnam
Bronze drums cast with spiral patterns and concentric bird-men. Traded across mainland Southeast Asia — a pre-Indic shared world.
680 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Sphinx of Taharqa
Nubian Kush · 25th Dynasty · c. 680 BCE
A Nubian pharaoh in the form of an Egyptian sphinx, carrying a double crown he had earned as conqueror. Now in the British Museum.
650 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Pyramids of Nuri
Napatan Kush · c. 650–300 BCE · Sudan
The burial field of Taharqa, last of the Kushite Black Pharaohs, and twenty of his successors. Recent underwater excavations have plunged archaeologists into flooded burial chambers still partially filled with gilded coffin ornaments and shawabtis.
650 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Ziwiye Hoard
Scythian/Median · 7th century BCE · Kurdistan, Iran
A spectacular cache of gold, silver, and ivory found in a bronze coffin in 1947 — or so the story goes; much of it was dispersed on the black market. Surviving pieces hybridize Scythian animal style with Assyrian and Urartian imagery.
645 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Lion Hunt of Ashurbanipal
Neo-Assyrian · c. 645 BCE · Nineveh
Alabaster reliefs from the king's palace. A dying lioness drags paralyzed hind legs — one of the most unnervingly precise animal studies in ancient art.
600 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Gayer-Anderson Cat
Late Period · c. 600 BCE
A hollow-cast bronze cat in the form of the goddess Bastet, with gold earrings and nose ring. Slightly battered but uncannily alive — one of the most reproduced pieces in the British Museum.
600 BCE
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Ban Chiang
Bronze Age · 1500 BCE – 300 CE · Thailand
Painted red-on-buff pottery from a prehistoric village in northeast Thailand — swirling spirals and concentric whorls. Bronze was cast here earlier than the textbooks once allowed.
590 BCE
Mediterranean
New York Kouros
Archaic Greek · c. 590 BCE
One of the earliest freestanding Greek marble nudes, Attic. Still stiff with Egyptian influence — striding left foot, clenched fists — but the archaic smile has arrived.
575 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Ishtar Gate
Neo-Babylonian · c. 575 BCE
Glazed brick gate to Babylon under Nebuchadnezzar II. Dragons and bulls in cobalt blue. Reconstructed in Berlin's Pergamon Museum.
570 BCE
Mediterranean
François Vase
by Kleitias & Ergotimos
Attic black-figure · c. 570 BCE · signed Kleitias/Ergotimos
A volute krater dense with 270 named figures in six stacked registers — Achilles ambushing Troilus, the marriage of Peleus and Thetis, the Calydonian boar hunt. Found in fragments in an Etruscan tomb near Chiusi in 1844.
560 BCE
Mediterranean
Moschophoros (Calf Bearer)
Archaic Greek · c. 560 BCE · Acropolis
A man carries a calf to sacrifice; both smile — the archaic grin. His tunic is incised rather than carved, an early convention already on its way out.
550 BCE
Americas
Chavín de Huántar
Chavín · 900–200 BCE · Peru
A mountain temple with underground galleries and the Lanzón, a carved granite blade — Peru's oldest pan-Andean god, still in his original chamber.
539 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Cyrus Cylinder
Achaemenid Persia · c. 539 BCE
A clay cylinder inscribed after Cyrus took Babylon. Often called the first charter of human rights — a claim the British Museum treats with more caution.
530 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Tomb of Cyrus at Pasargadae
Achaemenid Persia · c. 530 BCE · Iran
A simple stepped stone tomb for the founder of the empire. Alexander, visiting 200 years later, found it looted and ordered it restored.
530 BCE
Mediterranean
Kroisos Kouros
Archaic Greek · c. 530 BCE
A young man stepping forward, hands at his sides, slight smile — the archaic formula. Found on a grave at Anavyssos, one leg at a time.
520 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Temple of Hibis
Late Period · 6th c. BCE · Kharga Oasis
The largest surviving temple of the Persian period in Egypt. Carved in sandstone in the middle of a desert oasis; its roof blocks depict 700 Egyptian deities.
520 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Behistun Inscription
Achaemenid Persia · 520 BCE · Iran
Darius the Great's autobiography carved three hundred feet up a cliff, in three languages. The trilingual key that let Rawlinson decipher cuneiform.
520 BCE
Mediterranean
Sarcophagus of the Spouses
Etruscan · c. 520 BCE · Italy
A painted terracotta couple reclining together on their tomb lid — smiling, touching, treated as equals. Unthinkable in contemporary Greek or Egyptian funerary art.
518 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Persepolis
Achaemenid Persia · 518 BCE onward · Iran
Ceremonial capital of Darius and Xerxes. Reliefs show envoys from every corner of the empire bringing tribute. Burned by Alexander in 330 BCE.
515 BCE
Mediterranean
Euphronios Krater
by Euphronios
Archaic Greek · Euphronios &amp; Euxitheos · c. 515 BCE
A red-figure calyx-krater showing Hypnos and Thanatos carrying Sarpedon's body. Looted from an Etruscan tomb; caught up in a 30-year dispute; finally returned to Italy in 2008.
510 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Frieze of the Archers of Darius I
c. 510 BCE · Glazed brick · Richelieu Wing
From the palace of Darius the Great at Susa. A procession of the king's elite guard — the "Immortals" — in brilliantly colored glazed brick. Each carries a spear, bow, and quiver; each face is subtly different from the next.
510 BCE
Mediterranean
Apollo of Veii
by attributed to Vulca
Etruscan · c. 510 BCE · Veii
A full-size polychrome terracotta from the roofline of the Portonaccio sanctuary, striding forward to contest Herakles for a stolen deer. Its broad smile and swinging drapery put Etruscan sculpture at parity with anything being made in archaic Greece.
500 BCE
Africa
Nok Terracotta
Nok culture · 500 BCE – 500 CE · Nigeria
Hollow terracotta heads with triangular eyes and pierced pupils. The oldest known figurative sculpture in sub-Saharan Africa.
500 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Apadana Reliefs
Achaemenid Persia · c. 500 BCE · Persepolis
The great staircase of the audience hall. Subject peoples from 23 nations process forward with tribute — Ethiopians with ivory, Lydians with bowls, Bactrian camels.
500 BCE
Mediterranean
Temple of Aphaia
Late Archaic Greek · c. 500 BCE · Aegina
A limestone temple whose pedimental sculptures, now in Munich, capture the hinge between Archaic stiffness and Classical naturalism — look at the east and west together to see a style changing.
500 BCE
Mediterranean
Roman Forum
Roman Republic → Imperial · 7th c. BCE – 4th c. CE
The civic heart of ancient Rome — law courts, speaker's platforms, temples, triumphal arches, all crammed into a low strip between the Palatine and Capitoline hills.
500 BCE
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Plain of Jars
Iron Age · 500 BCE – 500 CE · Laos
Thousands of carved stone jars scattered across a plateau. Probably funerary; ground-truth research has been slow because the plain is still littered with unexploded American bombs.
500 BCE
Americas
Nazca Lines
Nazca culture · 500 BCE – 500 CE · Peru
Hummingbirds, monkeys, and spiders drawn at the scale of football fields by clearing pebbles from the desert floor. Visible only from the air.
500 BCE
Americas
Monte Albán
Zapotec · 500 BCE – 850 CE · Oaxaca
A leveled mountaintop city with one of the earliest writing systems in the Americas. Its danzantes reliefs show contorted, stripped captives.
500 BCE
Americas
Raimondi Stele
Chavín · c. 500 BCE · Peru
A two-meter granite slab carved with the Staff God, his elaborate headdress readable in more than one direction — right-side up he is a fanged deity, upside down a different snarling face emerges from the headdress. The ambiguity is programmatic.
480 BCE
Mediterranean
Kritios Boy
Early Classical Greek · c. 480 BCE
The hinge between Archaic and Classical — the first marble body where weight shifts onto one leg (contrapposto), giving the figure life.
480 BCE
Mediterranean
Tomb of the Leopards
Etruscan painted tomb · c. 480 BCE · Tarquinia
A small banquet chamber whose back wall shows two leopards flanking a lintel, below which men recline on couches while flute and lyre players serenade them. The brilliant red-and-black palette and easy joy have no parallel in surviving Greek painting.
480 BCE
Mediterranean
Tomb of the Diver
Greek colonial · c. 480 BCE · Paestum
The only known example of Greek figural tomb painting from the classical period. A young man dives into water beneath a lone tree — possibly a metaphor for the leap into the afterlife; the side walls show a genial symposium underway.
478 BCE
Mediterranean
Charioteer of Delphi
Early Classical · c. 478 BCE
One of the few surviving Greek bronzes. His calm expression, inset with glass and copper eyes, is a moment in a victory lap — all force held still.
470 BCE
Mediterranean
Ludovisi Throne
Greek · c. 470 BCE
A marble three-sided relief of uncertain function (a throne? an altar?). On its main panel, Aphrodite rising from the sea, draped in a wet gown that might as well not be there.
470 BCE
Mediterranean
Temple of Zeus at Olympia
Classical Greek · c. 470 BCE
Once held the chryselephantine Zeus — one of the Seven Wonders of the ancient world, carved by Phidias. The temple fell in an earthquake; the Zeus was hauled to Constantinople and burned.
460 BCE
Mediterranean
Artemision Bronze
Classical Greek · c. 460 BCE
Pulled from a Roman shipwreck off Cape Artemision. Either Zeus throwing a thunderbolt or Poseidon throwing a trident — his missing weapon makes it impossible to say.
455 BCE
Mediterranean
Riace Bronzes
Classical Greek · c. 460–450 BCE · Italy
Two life-size bronze warriors pulled from the sea off Calabria in 1972. Their teeth are silver, their eyelashes hammered copper.
450 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Naqsh-e Rustam
Achaemenid & Sasanian · 5th c. BCE – 3rd c. CE · Iran
Cruciform tombs cut high into a cliff for Darius the Great and his heirs. Centuries later the Sasanians carved their own triumphs below.
450 BCE
Mediterranean
Discobolus
by Myron
Classical Greek · c. 450 BCE (Roman copy)
Myron's discus thrower — the original bronze is lost; only Roman marble copies survive. A moment just before release, coiled and still.
450 BCE
Mediterranean
Capitoline Wolf
Etruscan or Medieval bronze · 5th c. BCE or 13th c. CE
The she-wolf nursing Romulus and Remus — emblem of Rome. Once thought ancient Etruscan; recent analysis suggests a medieval re-casting.
440 BCE
Mediterranean
Doryphoros
by Polykleitos
Classical Greek · Polykleitos · c. 440 BCE
The "Spear Bearer" — a treatise on ideal human proportion made in marble. Polykleitos wrote a book called the Canon about it; only this statue survives.
440 BCE
Mediterranean
Parthenon Frieze
Classical Greek · Pheidias workshop · c. 440 BCE
A continuous band 160 meters long, showing the Panathenaic procession — horses, musicians, elders, gods receiving the city's tribute. Half survives in the British Museum, half in Athens.
440 BCE
Mediterranean
Temple of Poseidon at Sounion
Classical Greek · c. 440 BCE · Attica
White marble columns on a cape where the sea drops 60 meters away on three sides. Byron carved his name into one of the columns in 1810.
439 BCE
Mediterranean
The Parthenon
Classical Greek · 447–432 BCE · Athens
Temple to Athena on the Acropolis. The columns lean inward and swell slightly in the middle — optical corrections so they look perfectly straight.
420 BCE
Mediterranean
Nike of Paionios
by Paionios
Classical Greek · Paionios · c. 420 BCE · Olympia
The Messenians and Naupactians dedicated her at Olympia after a victory over Sparta. Nike lands on a plinth, wind pressing drapery against her body — 170 years before the Winged Victory of Samothrace.
413 BCE
Mediterranean
Erechtheion
Classical Greek · 421–406 BCE · Athens
Temple on the sacred north side of the Acropolis. Six marble maidens (the Caryatids) hold up the porch in place of columns.
400 BCE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Oxus Treasure
Achaemenid Persia · 5th–4th c. BCE · Tajikistan
180 gold and silver objects washed out of a riverbed — chariots, lions, votive plaques. The British Museum's greatest hoard of Achaemenid metalwork.
400 BCE
Mediterranean
Chimera of Arezzo
Etruscan · c. 400 BCE
Bronze of the mythical lion-goat-snake beast, wounded and snarling. Found in 1553 and immediately cleaned up by Cellini; the inscription on its leg is Etruscan, not Latin.
400 BCE
Americas
Tikal
Classic Maya · 400 BCE – 900 CE · Guatemala
Step pyramids rising above the rainforest canopy. A Maya superpower that swallowed rival cities until a long drought took it down.
360 BCE
Mediterranean
Aphrodite of Knidos
by Praxiteles
Classical Greek · Praxiteles · c. 360 BCE
The first free-standing nude female figure in Greek sculpture — scandalous enough that men climbed her sanctuary walls at night. Only copies survive.
350 BCE
Mediterranean
Lady of Elche
Iberian · c. 4th c. BCE · Spain
A limestone bust with enormous spoked headdress-wheels over the ears. Iberian, not Greek — the style belongs to a world that left no writing to explain itself.
350 BCE
Mediterranean
Mausoleum at Halicarnassus
Greek · c. 350 BCE · Turkey
Tomb of the Carian satrap Mausolus, so grand that his name became the word for any lavish tomb. Wonder of the ancient world; shaken down by earthquakes; its fragments in the British Museum.
330 BCE
Mediterranean
Apoxyomenos
by Lysippos
Classical Greek · Lysippos · c. 330 BCE
An athlete scraping oil from his arm. The Roman marble copy stands in for a lost bronze original by Alexander the Great's favorite sculptor.
300 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Pyramids of Meroë
Kingdom of Kush · 300 BCE – 300 CE · Sudan
Over 200 pyramids, steeper and smaller than Egypt's, built for Nubian royalty. Sudan has more pyramids than Egypt — about twice as many.
300 BCE
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Moon of Pejeng
Dong Son tradition · c. 300 BCE · Bali
The largest bronze kettledrum in the world — almost two meters across. In Balinese legend it's the fallen wheel of a chariot of the moon.
250 BCE
South Asia
Great Stupa at Sanchi
Mauryan → Shunga · 3rd c. BCE onward
A hemispherical mound containing relics of the Buddha. The four carved gateways around it narrate his life without ever depicting him directly.
250 BCE
South Asia
Lion Capital of Ashoka
Mauryan · c. 250 BCE · Sarnath
Four back-to-back lions atop a polished sandstone column, once topped by a wheel. Adopted as the national emblem of India in 1950.
250 BCE
South Asia
Rampurva Bull Capital
Mauryan · 3rd c. BCE · Bihar
An Ashokan pillar capital: a single polished Chunar sandstone bull, so highly burnished it was mistaken for metal. One of the finest survivals of the Mauryan court style.
250 BCE
Americas
Paracas Textile
Paracas · c. 300–200 BCE · Peru
Embroidered mummy bundles wrapped in layer after layer of cotton and camelid fiber — winged shamans, deities, severed heads — colors still bright after two millennia in the Paracas desert.
226 BCE
Americas
Palenque
Classic Maya · 226 BCE – 799 CE · Chiapas
A small Maya kingdom with exquisite stucco portraits and one of the most carefully decoded royal tombs in the Americas — that of Pakal the Great.
225 BCE
Mediterranean
Dying Gaul
Hellenistic · c. 230–220 BCE (Roman copy)
A defeated Celt collapsing on his shield, wound dripping. Pergamene art showed the enemy as human — a compliment paid in marble, after the kill.
220 BCE
Mediterranean
Barberini Faun
Hellenistic · c. 220 BCE
A sleeping satyr sprawled drunk on a rock, legs splayed, utterly without dignity. Excavated from Hadrian's mausoleum moat — perhaps thrown over the walls during a siege.
210 BCE
East Asia
Terracotta Army
Qin dynasty · 210 BCE · Xi'an, China
Over 8,000 soldiers, each with a different face, buried with the first emperor of China to guard him after death. Farmers digging a well found them in 1974.
200 BCE
Mediterranean
Winged Victory of Samothrace
Hellenistic · c. 200 BCE
Nike landing on the prow of a ship, wind pressing her drapery against her body. One of the most physically dynamic pieces of ancient sculpture.
196 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Rosetta Stone
Ptolemaic · 196 BCE
A decree written three times — in hieroglyphs, Demotic, and Greek — that became the key to reading Egyptian. Champollion cracked it in 1822.
170 BCE
Mediterranean
Pergamon Altar
Hellenistic · c. 180–160 BCE · Pergamon
A colossal marble frieze of the gods fighting the giants — deep-cut writhing bodies that may have inspired Michelangelo. The whole structure was hauled to Berlin in 1880.
150 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Kom Ombo
Ptolemaic · 2nd c. BCE
A rare double temple — one half for the crocodile god Sobek, the other for Horus the Elder. Mummified crocodiles still fill a side chamber.
150 BCE
Mediterranean
House of the Faun
Roman · 2nd c. BCE · Pompeii
One of the largest residences in Pompeii, three acres across. Its atrium held the bronze dancing faun that gave it the name; the floor held the Alexander Mosaic.
150 BCE
South Asia
Ajanta Caves
Buddhist · 2nd c. BCE – 6th c. CE · Maharashtra
Thirty rock-cut monasteries with surviving murals of the Buddha's past lives. Abandoned, forgotten, and rediscovered by a tiger-hunting British officer in 1819.
150 BCE
South Asia
Bhaja Caves
Early Buddhist · 2nd c. BCE · Maharashtra
Twenty-two rock-cut viharas and a chaitya, with wooden ribs imitated in stone. Reliefs show figures riding elephants, dancing — a rare look at secular imagery in early Buddhist art.
120 BCE
Mediterranean
Antikythera Mechanism
Hellenistic · c. 150–100 BCE · Greece
A corroded bronze device pulled from a Roman shipwreck in 1901. CT scans finally revealed: a geared orrery predicting sun, moon, and five planets. No surviving match until the Renaissance.
115 BCE
Mediterranean
Venus de Milo
Hellenistic Greek · c. 130–100 BCE
Marble Aphrodite found on the island of Milos in 1820. The missing arms are part of her fame — we don't know what she was doing.
100 BCE
Mediterranean
Boxer at Rest
Hellenistic · c. 100 BCE
A bronze boxer after a fight — cauliflower ears, bloodied lip, exhausted. One of the few surviving Greek originals, not a later copy.
100 BCE
Mediterranean
Alexander Mosaic
Roman copy of Hellenistic painting · c. 100 BCE · Pompeii
Alexander charging Darius at Issus, rendered in a million tiny tesserae. The lost original oil painting was probably by Philoxenos of Eretria.
100 BCE
Mediterranean
Borghese Gladiator
by Agasias of Ephesus
c. 100 BCE · Marble · Sully Wing
Not actually a gladiator — a warrior fighting a mounted opponent, arm raised with a (lost) shield. A tour de force of anatomical study; every muscle is taut with the effort of the moment before impact.
100 BCE
Americas
Teotihuacan
Pre-Aztec · 100 BCE – 550 CE · Mexico
A city of over 100,000 at its peak — larger than Rome at the same time. Already abandoned when the Aztecs found it and named it "the place where gods were born."
60 BCE
Mediterranean
Villa of the Mysteries
Roman frescoed villa · c. 60 BCE · Pompeii
Life-size figures on a crimson ground enact what looks like the initiation of a bride into a Dionysian cult — a flagellation, a satyr's mask, a winged figure about to whip a kneeling woman. No one is sure whether the scene is myth, rite, or both.
54 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Dendera Temple
Ptolemaic → Roman · 54 BCE – 20 CE
Temple of Hathor. Its astronomical ceilings include the only known circular zodiac from ancient Egypt — now a replica; the original is in the Louvre.
50 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Dendera Zodiac
Ptolemaic · c. 50 BCE
A circular sandstone relief of the night sky carried off from the ceiling of Dendera temple by a French explorer in 1821. Now in the Louvre; a replica hangs in the original.
50 BCE
Mediterranean
Laocoön and His Sons
Hellenistic · 1st c. BCE – 1st c. CE
A Trojan priest and his sons strangled by sea serpents. Dug up in a vineyard in 1506 and hauled to the Vatican. Michelangelo studied it for weeks.
50 BCE
Mediterranean
Boy with Thorn
Hellenistic → Roman · 1st c. BCE (later copies)
A boy pulling a thorn from his foot, head bent in total concentration. One of the first ancient bronzes to be widely copied in the Renaissance.
50 BCE
South Asia
Karla Caves
Early Buddhist · 1st c. BCE · Maharashtra
The largest rock-cut chaitya hall in India — a barrel-vaulted cave church with a lantern roof carved in stone to mimic wood beams it never had.
15 BCE
Egypt & Nubia
Temple of Dendur
Roman Egypt · 15 BCE
A small sandstone temple given to the United States in 1965 when Lake Nasser was about to drown it. Reassembled stone by stone inside the Met.
11 BCE
Mediterranean
Ara Pacis
Augustan Rome · 13–9 BCE
The Altar of Peace. The imperial family processes in carved marble, framed by acanthus tendrils. Augustus inserts himself into the mythic past.

0 – 500 CE49 works

0 CE
Africa
San Rock Art
San · up to 30,000 years old · Southern Africa
Eland, hunters, and trance figures painted on rock shelters from Namibia to the Drakensberg. One of humanity's longest unbroken painting traditions.
0 CE
Africa
Brandberg Rock Paintings
San · up to 2,000 years old · Namibia
The Brandberg Massif hides thousands of rock paintings, most famously the "White Lady" — not a lady at all, but a medicine figure. Painted for ritual, not ornament.
0 CE
Africa
Twyfelfontein Petroglyphs
Pre-San hunter-gatherers · 6,000 years old · Namibia
Thousands of rock engravings of giraffes, rhinos, and lions on sandstone. A seal, 900 km from the nearest sea, suggests long-distance travel — or long memory.
0 CE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Petra — Al-Khazneh
Nabataean · 1st c. BCE – 1st c. CE · Jordan
The "Treasury" — a 40-meter temple-facade carved directly into the rose-red sandstone cliff. Bedouin shot at the urn at the top, hoping to spill hidden pharaoh's gold.
0 CE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Temple of Anahita at Kangavar
Parthian · 2nd century BCE–2nd century CE · western Iran
A vast rectangular terrace edged with columns carved in a hybrid Greco-Persian style, dedicated to the water goddess Anahita. Its scale — more than 200 meters long — and hellenistic detailing testify to the Parthian synthesis after Alexander.
0 CE
South Asia
Bhimbetka Rock Shelters
Mesolithic onward · 30,000 BCE – historical · India
Painted caves with some of the oldest hunting scenes on earth — a human riding a gigantic red animal, drawn in ochre and charcoal.
0 CE
South Asia
Amaravati Stupa Reliefs
Satavahana · 2nd c. BCE – 3rd c. CE · Andhra Pradesh
A Buddhist stupa whose limestone casing was carved with some of the earliest Buddha figures in India. Most pieces are now in the British Museum and the Chennai Government Museum.
0 CE
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Ubirr Rock Art
Aboriginal Australian · up to 20,000 years old · Kakadu
One of the oldest continuous art traditions on earth. Later paintings show fish anatomy so precisely that species are still identifiable.
2 CE
Mediterranean
Maison Carrée
Roman temple · c. 2 CE · Nîmes
The best-preserved Roman temple anywhere, built by Agrippa for the imperial cult. Thomas Jefferson obsessed over its proportions and based the Virginia State Capitol on it, launching the Roman revival in American civic architecture.
13 CE
Mediterranean
Portland Vase
Roman cameo glass · c. 1–25 CE
A cobalt-blue glass vase dipped in white and carved back through the outer layer. One of the finest surviving pieces of Roman cameo glass — and inspiration for Wedgwood's first portraits.
15 CE
Mediterranean
Augustus of Prima Porta
Early Roman Empire · c. 15 CE
The emperor posed as a general, barefoot like a god. The breastplate shows the return of captured Roman standards — a diplomatic win dressed as conquest.
50 CE
Mediterranean
Pont du Gard
Roman aqueduct · 1st century CE · southern France
A three-tiered limestone aqueduct carrying water fifty kilometers to Nîmes across the Gardon valley. Each block weighs up to six tonnes and was set without mortar; the gradient over the full course drops only 12.6 meters.
70 CE
Mediterranean
Pula Arena
Roman amphitheater · 1st century CE · Istria, Croatia
One of only six surviving Roman amphitheaters that retain a complete outer wall. Seating 23,000 for gladiatorial combat, the limestone ring still hosts opera and concerts under the open sky each summer.
75 CE
Mediterranean
The Colosseum
Flavian · 70–80 CE · Rome
An elliptical amphitheater for 50,000 spectators. The underground hypogeum ran elevators, flooded the floor for sea battles, and housed wild animals.
79 CE
Mediterranean
Pompeii
Roman · buried 79 CE
Vesuvius sealed a whole Roman town in volcanic ash. Frescoes, bread loaves, graffiti, and the shapes of bodies all survived — daily life frozen.
79 CE
Mediterranean
Herculaneum
Roman · buried 79 CE · Italy
Pompeii's smaller, richer neighbor. Volcanic mud sealed it instead of ash, so wooden beams, baby cradles, and carbonized scrolls all survived.
81 CE
Mediterranean
Arch of Titus
Roman Imperial · 81 CE · Rome
Triumphal arch for the sack of Jerusalem. Its inner relief shows the Menorah and other temple spoils being carried in procession — our earliest image of the Second Temple's contents.
100 CE
Americas
San Agustín Stone Figures
San Agustín culture · 100 BCE – 800 CE · Colombia
Over 500 monolithic statues of guardians, jaguar-men, and birth scenes, scattered across an Andean plateau. Who the carvers were, or why they stopped, remains unknown.
113 CE
Mediterranean
Trajan's Column
Imperial Roman · 113 CE
A 100-foot marble column whose spiral frieze narrates the Dacian Wars in 155 continuous scenes — history carved as a comic strip.
125 CE
Mediterranean
Library of Celsus at Ephesus
Roman Imperial · 110–135 CE · Turkey
Two-story facade of a library that held 12,000 scrolls and the sarcophagus of its founder in a chamber behind the wall — a library as a tomb.
126 CE
Mediterranean
Pantheon
Roman Imperial · 126 CE · Rome
The largest unreinforced concrete dome ever built. The oculus at its center has been open to the sky for nineteen centuries — rain falls through it into drains in the floor.
127 CE
Mediterranean
Hadrian's Villa
Roman Imperial · 117–138 CE · Tivoli
A 250-acre retreat where the emperor built scale models of the sights from his tour of the empire — a Nile canal, an Athenian stoa, a Canopus.
130 CE
Mediterranean
Apollo Belvedere
Hellenistic / Roman copy · c. 120–140 CE
A marble copy of a lost Greek bronze, long considered the pinnacle of male beauty. Winckelmann helped launch Neoclassicism by writing about this single statue.
150 CE
Egypt & Nubia
Fayum Mummy Portraits
Roman Egypt · 1st–3rd c. CE
Encaustic panels placed over the faces of mummies. The most personal, most realistic portraits to survive from the ancient world — they still look you in the eye.
150 CE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Hatra
Parthian · 1st–3rd c. CE · Iraq
A desert caravan city whose kings commissioned limestone statues fusing Hellenistic drapery with Parthian dress. Destroyed by ISIS in 2015.
150 CE
Mediterranean
Sleeping Hermaphroditus
Roman copy of a Greek original · 2nd century CE · Sully Wing
From one angle, a sleeping woman. Walk around to the other side: the body is both. The mattress beneath — realistic enough that you want to touch it — was carved by Bernini in 1619 when the statue was restored.
150 CE
South Asia
Mathura Bodhisattva
Kushan · 1st–3rd c. CE · Uttar Pradesh
Red sandstone figures in a distinctly Indian idiom — broad-shouldered, loose-robed, flatter and more frontal than the Greco-Buddhist Gandhara school evolving to the northwest.
175 CE
Mediterranean
Equestrian Statue of Marcus Aurelius
Roman Imperial · c. 175 CE
The only surviving large Roman bronze equestrian. It lived because Christians mistook it for Constantine. Now replaced by a copy in the Campidoglio; the original is inside.
200 CE
Mediterranean
Leptis Magna
Roman provincial city · expanded 193 CE · Libya
Hometown of Septimius Severus and the showpiece of Roman Africa, with its four-way arch, Severan basilica, and enormous forum paved in imported marble. Desert sand buried and preserved it after the Arab conquest.
200 CE
Americas
Pyramid of the Sun
Teotihuacan · c. 200 CE · Mexico
The third-largest pyramid in the world by volume, built over a natural cave that was probably the origin myth's portal to the underworld. The builders of Teotihuacan are unknown — the city had fallen five centuries before the Aztec arrived to rename it.
200 CE
Americas
Pyramid of the Moon
Teotihuacan · c. 200 CE · Mexico
Smaller than the Pyramid of the Sun but terminating the Avenue of the Dead in its shadow of Cerro Gordo. Archaeologists tunneling into it have found successive burial caches with jade figurines, obsidian blades, and sacrificed wolves.
216 CE
Mediterranean
Baths of Caracalla
Roman imperial · 216 CE · Rome
A 25-hectare bathing complex that could hold 1,600 people at once, heated by underfloor hypocausts stoked by armies of slaves. The painted vaults fell centuries ago but Shelley wrote much of Prometheus Unbound wandering the ruins.
237 CE
Egypt & Nubia
Temple of Edfu
Ptolemaic · 237–57 BCE
The best-preserved temple in Egypt, because it was buried in desert sand for centuries. Dedicated to Horus, with reliefs of his battle against Seth.
250 CE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Naqsh-e Rajab
Sasanian · 3rd c. CE · Iran
Rock reliefs near Persepolis celebrating the founder of the Sasanian empire. Ardashir receives the crown directly from Ahura Mazda — politics cast as theology.
250 CE
South Asia
Gandhara Buddha
Greco-Buddhist · 1st–5th c. CE · Pakistan/Afghanistan
The first sculptural images of the Buddha, made in schist and draped in togas. A crossroads style born from Alexander's successors.
260 CE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Colossus of Shapur I
Sasanian · 3rd century CE · Bishapur cave, Iran
A seven-meter standing figure carved from a stalagmite deep in a cave above Bishapur. Shapur stands in full regalia, his thick beard curled and mustaches pointing sideways in the signature Sasanian style; fragments of his arms were reassembled in the twentieth century.
266 CE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Bishapur
Sasanian royal city · 266 CE · Fars, Iran
Built by Shapur I to commemorate his victory over three Roman emperors, including the capture of Valerian. Its grid plan drew on Antioch; its palace mosaics were pure Roman workmanship executed by prisoners-of-war.
290 CE
Americas
Lord of Sipán Tomb
Moche · c. 290 CE · Peru
The intact grave of a Moche warrior-priest, draped in gold, silver, turquoise, and Spondylus. Discovered in 1987 moments ahead of looters.
305 CE
Mediterranean
Diocletian's Palace
Late Roman · 305 CE · Split, Croatia
A retired emperor's fortified seaside retreat built on the plan of a military camp. Its peristyle, mausoleum (now the cathedral), and substructures form the kernel around which the medieval city of Split grew.
315 CE
Mediterranean
Arch of Constantine
Late Roman · 315 CE · Rome
A triumphal arch cobbled together from reused second-century reliefs of Trajan, Hadrian, and Marcus Aurelius — a frank admission that fourth-century sculptors could no longer match the earlier skill. The recycled figures were even recut with Constantine's head.
350 CE
Africa
Obelisks of Aksum
Aksumite kingdom · 4th c. · Ethiopia
Granite stelae marking royal graves; the tallest once stood nearly a hundred feet high. One was looted by Mussolini, returned in 2005.
353 CE
East Asia
Lantingji Xu (Preface to the Orchid Pavilion)
by Wang Xizhi
Eastern Jin · Wang Xizhi · 353 CE · China
A drunkenly perfect calligraphic masterpiece, 324 characters describing a riverside gathering of poets. Wang Xizhi tried to rewrite it next day and could not match it. Later buried with an emperor.
380 CE
East Asia
Admonitions Scroll
by Gu Kaizhi
Six Dynasties · Gu Kaizhi (copy) · c. 380 CE
A handscroll of court ladies illustrating a Confucian poem of proper conduct. Probably the oldest surviving Chinese narrative painting, preserved in a Tang-era copy.
400 CE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Taq Kasra
Sasanian · 3rd–6th c. CE · Iraq
The largest single-span brick vault in the ancient world — the throne hall of the Sasanian palace at Ctesiphon. It still stands, barely.
400 CE
East Asia
Nymph of the Luo River
by Gu Kaizhi
Gu Kaizhi (attr.) · c. 400 CE · Song copy at the Palace Museum, Beijing
A handscroll illustrating Cao Zhi's poem about his unrequited love for a river nymph. Figures float on waves rendered in iron-wire line, backgrounds compressed into pictographic mountains — an early statement of the Chinese approach to narrative through suggestion.
400 CE
Americas
Moche Culture
Moche · 100–700 CE · Northern Peru
Before the Inca, the Moche built painted pyramids and made portrait pots so individual you can tell one person from another across fifteen hundred years.
477 CE
South Asia
Sigiriya
Anuradhapura period · 477 CE · Sri Lanka
A palace on top of a 660-foot granite plug. Its western face once carried 500 paintings of celestial women; twenty-one still survive.
492 CE
East Asia
Yungang Grottoes
Northern Wei · 460–525 CE · Datong
Fifty-three caves with 51,000 statues. The earliest cliff Buddhas in China — faces still showing Indian and Central Asian lineage.
493 CE
East Asia
Longmen Grottoes
Northern Wei → Tang · 493 CE onward · Luoyang
Over 100,000 Buddhas carved into limestone cliffs along the Yi River. The largest is fifty-six feet tall and smiles.

500 – 100072 works

500 CE
Africa
Lydenburg Heads
Early Iron Age · 500 CE · South Africa
Terracotta heads found buried under a farm in Mpumalanga — the earliest known African figurative sculpture south of the equator.
500 CE
Mesopotamia & Persia
Taq-e Bostan
Sasanian · 4th–7th century CE · Kermanshah, Iran
Rock-cut grottoes carved into a cliff at a sacred spring, showing Sasanian kings receiving divine investiture and hunting boar from reed-filled marshes. The larger grotto's royal boar-hunt panel is among the most elaborate narrative reliefs in Iranian art.
500 CE
South Asia
Dhamek Stupa
Gupta era · 500 CE · Sarnath
Solid brick stupa marking the spot of the Buddha's first sermon. Its upper band of geometric floral carving is some of the finest Gupta ornament anywhere.
500 CE
South Asia
Dashavatara Temple, Deogarh
Gupta · c. 500 CE · Uttar Pradesh
One of the earliest surviving Hindu stone temples, a compact cube topped by a now-damaged tower. Its relief panels — Vishnu asleep on Ananta, Nara-Narayana, the rescue of the elephant king Gajendra — are foundational images of Hindu narrative art.
500 CE
South Asia
Swayambhunath
Newar · c. 500 CE · Kathmandu
The 'monkey temple' atop a hill in the Kathmandu Valley, reached by 365 steps flanked by polychrome Buddhas. A 2015 earthquake split several subsidiary shrines; the main stupa, its painted eyes impassive, rode out the shaking.
500 CE
East Asia
Crown of Silla
Silla kingdom · 5th–6th c. · Korea
Thin gold sheet cut into tree and antler forms, hung with comma-shaped jades and gold spangles. Too fragile to wear — meant for ritual and burial.
500 CE
East Asia
Haniwa
Kofun period · 3rd–6th c. CE · Japan
Hollow terracotta cylinders and figures set in rings around imperial tumuli. Armored warriors, horses, dancers, shrine maidens — the only accessible record of pre-literate Japanese dress.
500 CE
Americas
Huaca de la Luna
Moche · c. 100–800 CE · Peru
A staggered adobe pyramid rebuilt in six successive phases, each burying the previous in a deliberate act of renewal. Inner walls were painted with giant Ai Apaec decapitator gods in red, white, yellow, and black — pigments that survived because each phase sealed the previous art beneath fresh bricks.
537 CE
Islamic World
Hagia Sophia
Byzantine & Ottoman · 537 CE onward · Istanbul
A Byzantine cathedral that held the largest dome on earth for a thousand years, then an Ottoman mosque, then a museum, now a mosque again.
550 CE
Africa
Debre Damo Monastery
Aksumite · 6th century · Ethiopia
Perched atop a flat-topped mountain reachable only by a 15-meter rope hauled up by the monks. Its interior preserves carved wooden coffers and rafters that are the oldest surviving examples of Aksumite architecture.
570 CE
South Asia
Buddhas of Bamiyan
Greco-Buddhist · 6th c. · Afghanistan
Two standing Buddhas, 38 and 55 meters tall, carved into a sandstone cliff on the Silk Road. The Taliban dynamited them in 2001; projection mapping now fills the empty niches on quiet nights.
600 CE
South Asia
Elephanta Caves
Kalachuri / Chalukya · 5th–8th c. · Mumbai
Rock-cut Shiva temple on an island. The Trimurti — Shiva as creator, preserver, and destroyer — emerges twenty feet tall from the living stone.
607 CE
East Asia
Hōryū-ji
Asuka period · c. 607 CE · Nara, Japan
Among the oldest wooden buildings still standing anywhere in the world. Built when Buddhism had only recently reached Japan.
610 CE
Americas
Copán
Classic Maya · 400–820 CE · Honduras
A small Maya kingdom with the finest stone sculpture in the Americas. Stelae-portraits of kings carved nearly in the round.
641 CE
East Asia
Emperor Taizong Receiving the Tibetan Envoy
by Yan Liben
Yan Liben · 641 CE · Palace Museum, Beijing
The Tang emperor receives Lu Dongzan, envoy of Tibetan King Songtsen Gampo, who came to arrange the royal marriage that would seal East Asian diplomacy for centuries. Yan Liben was court painter and prime minister simultaneously.
642 CE
South Asia
Jokhang Temple
Tibetan Buddhist · 642 CE · Lhasa
Tibet's holiest temple, founded by King Songtsen Gampo to house the Jowo Shakyamuni statue brought by his Tang Chinese wife. Pilgrims complete three prostrations every few steps around the Barkhor circuit outside; the flagstones are polished to a mirror.
650 CE
South Asia
Pancha Rathas
Pallava · 7th c. · Mahabalipuram
Five monolithic shrines carved from a single granite ridge in the shape of temple chariots. Each is a different architectural experiment — the prototypes for a millennium of South Indian temples.
691 CE
Islamic World
Dome of the Rock
Umayyad · 691 CE · Jerusalem
The earliest surviving major Islamic building. The gold dome marks the rock from which Muhammad is said to have ascended on the Night Journey.
700 CE
South Asia
Pattadakal
Chalukya dynasty · 7th–8th c. · Karnataka
A proving ground for Indian temple architecture — northern and southern styles built side by side for direct comparison before being exported across the subcontinent.
700 CE
Americas
Calakmul
Classic Maya · 5th–8th c. · Campeche
One of the two great Maya superpowers (with Tikal). A kingdom of 50,000 in a rainforest, its two largest pyramids rise 45 meters above the canopy.
705 CE
Islamic World
Al-Aqsa Mosque
Umayyad onward · 705 CE · Jerusalem
The congregational mosque on the Temple Mount, third holiest site in Islam. Rebuilt repeatedly after earthquakes; the wooden prayer hall facing the qibla dates partly to the 1930s.
710 CE
East Asia
Kōfuku-ji
Nara period onward · 710 · Japan
One of Nara's four great monasteries. Its treasure hall holds the Asura statue — a three-faced, six-armed youth with an achingly thoughtful face.
711 CE
East Asia
Fushimi Inari-taisha
Heian period onward · 711 CE · Kyoto
Ten thousand vermillion torii gates tunnel up a sacred mountain. Each is donated by a business praying for success; the writing on the back is the donor's name.
713 CE
East Asia
Leshan Giant Buddha
Tang China · 713–803 · Sichuan
A seated Maitreya carved directly into a sandstone cliff at the confluence of three rivers. Seventy-one meters tall; his ears alone are seven meters. Built, a monk said, to calm the waters.
715 CE
Islamic World
Umayyad Mosque of Damascus
Umayyad · 715 · Syria
Built on a site already sacred to Jupiter, John the Baptist, and Mary. Byzantine mosaicists were brought in to lay the gold-ground interior.
723 CE
Islamic World
Qusayr 'Amra
Umayyad · c. 723 · Jordan
A desert bath complex covered inside with frescoes of wrestlers, bathing women, and the six kings of the world — Caesar, Negus, Khosrow — defeated or soon to be defeated by Islam.
725 CE
South Asia
Shore Temple of Mahabalipuram
Pallava · c. 725 CE · Tamil Nadu
One of the earliest stone temples in South India. It stands on the Bay of Bengal; the 2004 tsunami briefly receded far enough to reveal more submerged temples.
743 CE
Islamic World
Mshatta Facade
Umayyad · 743–744 · Jordan
A desert palace facade dismantled and shipped to Berlin in 1903 as a diplomatic gift. Carved rosettes and winged lions in the softest yellow sandstone; too brittle to move today.
750 CE
Islamic World
Jameh Mosque of Isfahan
Seljuq onward · 8th c. – modern · Iran
A thousand years of Iranian architecture layered in one building — two of the greatest domes in Islamic history stand a few meters apart.
750 CE
East Asia
Night-Shining White
by Han Gan
Tang dynasty · Han Gan · c. 750 · China
A tethered stallion struggling at the post — snorting, hooves kicking. The emperor Xuanzong's favorite horse, painted in ink so alive it almost leaves the silk.
750 CE
East Asia
Spring Outing of the Tang Court
by Zhang Xuan
Tang Dynasty · attributed to Zhang Xuan · 8th c.
A later copy of a lost Zhang Xuan handscroll: the imperial court rides out on a spring morning in a tight file of horses. The women in the vanguard seem to pull the viewer along.
750 CE
Americas
Tiwanaku
Tiwanaku · 500–1000 CE · Bolivia
A highland capital on the shore of Lake Titicaca. Its Gate of the Sun — cut from a single stone — faces the point where the sun rises at equinox.
750 CE
Americas
Pyramid of the Magician
Late Classic Maya · 600–900 CE · Uxmal
An oval-based pyramid whose legend says it was built in one night by a dwarf magician. Every stone is carved; the central doorway is a monster's open mouth.
751 CE
East Asia
Bulguksa
Unified Silla · 751 CE · Gyeongju, Korea
A Buddhist temple with twin pagodas (Dabotap and Seokgatap) that encode two sutras in stone form. Burned by the Japanese in 1593, rebuilt in the 1970s on the original foundations.
751 CE
East Asia
Dabotap Pagoda
Unified Silla · 751 · Bulguksa, Korea
An ornate twin to the plainer Seokgatap across the courtyard. Two pagodas encoding the Lotus Sutra's doctrine that every Buddha is present in every other.
752 CE
East Asia
Tōdai-ji
Nara period · 752 · Japan
For a thousand years the largest wooden building in the world. Inside, a 50-foot bronze Buddha, cast in eight pieces, seated in a lotus.
756 CE
East Asia
Shōsōin Repository
Nara period · 756 CE · Nara, Japan
A log-cabin-style storehouse of Tōdai-ji that has preserved nine thousand personal belongings of Emperor Shōmu — lutes, masks, glass bowls from Sasanian Persia, a rhinoceros-horn comb — under near-perfect humidity for 1,300 years.
760 CE
South Asia
Kailasa Temple, Ellora
Rashtrakuta · c. 760 CE · Maharashtra
The largest monolithic sculpture on earth — a full Dravida-style temple carved top-down from a single basalt cliff, freeing perhaps two hundred thousand tonnes of rock. Krishna I's masons worked to a plan no draft of which survives; many believe none existed.
774 CE
East Asia
Seokguram Grotto
Unified Silla · 774 · Korea
A domed granite chamber holding a single seated Buddha, two and a half meters tall. The walls are carved with bodhisattvas and disciples — a sculptural mandala.
779 CE
South Asia
Samye Monastery
Tibetan Buddhist · 779 CE · U-Tsang
The first monastery built in Tibet, laid out as a three-dimensional mandala with the central temple representing Mount Meru and four chapels for the cosmic continents. Its founder Trisong Detsen imported Indian masters to ordain the first seven Tibetan monks.
784 CE
Islamic World
Mezquita of Córdoba
Umayyad of al-Andalus · 784 CE onward
A forest of red-and-white striped arches from a great caliphate. A cathedral was dropped into the middle of it in the 16th century.
790 CE
Americas
Bonampak Murals
Classic Maya · c. 790 CE · Chiapas
Three rooms painted wall to ceiling: a royal presentation, a battle, a victory dance ending in the torture of captives. The most complete Maya narrative painting to survive.
800 CE
Europe
Book of Kells
Insular Celtic · c. 800 CE · Ireland
A Latin gospel book from monks on Iona and Kells. The ornament is so intricate that Gerald of Wales thought it "the work of an angel, not of a man."
800 CE
South Asia
Nalanda Mahavihara
Pala dynasty · 5th–12th c. · Bihar
A Buddhist university that drew ten thousand monks from across Asia. Burned by Bakhtiyar Khalji around 1193; the library, they say, smoked for months.
800 CE
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Shwedagon Pagoda
Mon → Burmese · 6th–11th c. onward · Yangon
A gilded stupa 325 feet tall, covered in gold leaf and crowned with 4,000 diamonds. Said to enshrine hairs of the Buddha.
800 CE
Americas
Xochicalco
Epi-classic · c. 650–900 CE · Morelos, Mexico
A fortified mountaintop city that hosted what may have been an international calendar congress, depicted on the Pyramid of the Feathered Serpent's beautifully carved reliefs. An underground observatory tracks the sun through a vertical shaft at zenith passage.
836 CE
Islamic World
Great Mosque of Kairouan
Aghlabid · 836 · Tunisia
The oldest purpose-built mosque still standing. Its square minaret became the model for North African and Andalusi mosques for a thousand years.
850 CE
Africa
Igbo-Ukwu Bronze
Igbo · 9th c. · Nigeria
Lost-wax castings of startling delicacy — three centuries before Ife, and predating most European bronzework of the same technical sophistication.
850 CE
South Asia
Chand Baori
Gurjara-Pratihara · 9th c. · Rajasthan
A thirteen-story stepwell carved with 3,500 narrow steps into dizzying geometric patterns down its walls. One of the oldest and deepest stepwells in India.
850 CE
East Asia
Mogao Caves (Dunhuang)
Silk Road Buddhist · 4th–14th c. · Gansu
492 caves carved into cliff face, filled with murals and gilded clay Buddhas. One sealed chamber held 50,000 manuscripts — the largest trove of its kind.
850 CE
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Borobudur
Sailendra dynasty · 9th c. · Java, Indonesia
A stone mandala you walk. Climbing its nine terraces takes you through 2,672 relief panels and 504 Buddhas to the empty stupa at the top.
850 CE
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Prambanan
Medang Kingdom · 9th c. · Java, Indonesia
The Hindu counterpart to Borobudur a few miles away — a cluster of tall, sharp-edged temples to the Trimurti, walls carved with the Ramayana.
850 CE
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Mỹ Sơn
Champa Kingdom · 4th–14th c. · Vietnam
Brick Hindu temples in a jungle valley. Seventy stood before American bombs in 1969; twenty survive, still slowly giving up their secrets.
850 CE
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Wayang Kulit
Java · 9th c. onward · Indonesia
Flat leather shadow puppets of the Ramayana and Mahabharata, manipulated against a backlit screen. A traditional performance runs all night.
850 CE
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Balinese Temples
Balinese Hindu · 9th c. onward · Bali
