Every village has at least three: for the ancestors, for daily life, for the dead. Split gates, black palm-fiber roofs, statues wrapped in checkered cloth.
850 CE
Americas
Uxmal
Puuc Maya · c. 850 CE · Yucatán
Puuc-style architecture at its best, with mosaic façades of thousands of perfectly cut limestone panels forming rain god masks. The Pyramid of the Magician has uniquely rounded sides and the locals say it was raised in a single night by a dwarf.
851 CE
Islamic World
Great Mosque of Samarra
Abbasid · 851 · Iraq
Once the largest mosque in the world. Its Malwiya minaret spirals upward 170 feet — a ramp you climb on the outside.
867 CE
Europe
Byzantine Mosaics of Hagia Sophia
Byzantine · 867 CE onward · Constantinople
Figural mosaics on gold ground. The Virgin and Child in the apse, unveiled in 867 to reverse the Iconoclasm, glows from half a kilometer away.
879 CE
Islamic World
Mosque of Ibn Tulun
Tulunid · 879 · Cairo
The oldest mosque in Cairo still in its original form. A vast brick courtyard under a spiral minaret — a shape borrowed from Samarra.
900 CE
Americas
Chichen Itza
Post-Classic Maya · 600–1200 CE · Yucatán
On the spring equinox, sunlight strikes the pyramid's stairway to form a serpent that seems to slither down the temple of Kukulkan.
900 CE
Americas
El Caracol
Maya-Toltec · c. 900 CE · Chichen Itzá
A round astronomical observatory whose upper windows align with the extreme positions of Venus — a planet so important to the Maya that their calendars track it to the day. The ruined tower earned its name ('the snail') from its internal spiral staircase.
900 CE
Americas
El Tajín
Classic Veracruz · c. 600–1200 CE · Mexico
Its 'Pyramid of the Niches' has exactly 365 recessed square niches, one for each day of the solar year. Seventeen ball courts punctuate the site — carved with scenes of ritual decapitation that followed the game's conclusion.
925 CE
Americas
Quimbaya Goldwork
Quimbaya · 300–1550 CE · Colombia
Cast gold figures of caciques, poporos, and seated shamans. In 1892 a diplomat gave 122 of them to Spain — one of the largest pre-Columbian collections anywhere.
950 CE
Islamic World
Itchan Kala (Khiva)
Khanate of Khiva · 10th c. onward · Uzbekistan
The old inner town of Khiva — mud walls enclosing minarets, mausoleums, and palaces so untouched it can still look like a stage set.
950 CE
East Asia
Huang Quan Bird Painting
by Huang Quan
Five Dynasties · Huang Quan · c. 950 CE
Meticulously observed birds, insects, and turtles. Huang Quan was a palace painter in Sichuan; his "sketches from life" became the model for Northern Song nature painting.
950 CE
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Preah Vihear
Khmer Empire · 9th–11th c. · Cambodia
A cliff-top temple on the Thai border — 800 meters of causeway climbing to a sanctuary looking out over the plain. Thailand and Cambodia fought over its ownership in 2011.
956 CE
Islamic World
Medina Azahara
Caliphate of Córdoba · 936–976 · Spain
The palace-city built outside Córdoba for Abd al-Rahman III — marble floors, mercury pools, gardens that moved. Destroyed in civil war within a century of completion.
967 CE
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Banteay Srei
Khmer Empire · 967 · Cambodia
"Citadel of Women" — a small temple of pink sandstone carved with almost impossibly fine reliefs. Malraux tried to steal four figures in 1923 and was arrested.
968 CE
Islamic World
Pyxis of al-Mughira
968 CE · Carved ivory · Cour Visconti
A small ivory box made in Córdoba for a teenage Umayyad prince. Every surface is carved with figures — musicians, lions, lovers, a man stealing eggs from an eagle's nest. The inscriptions include barely-veiled warnings about the instability of royal succession. Al-Mughira was executed by strangulation eight years later.
970 CE
East Asia
Night Revels of Han Xizai
by Gu Hongzhong
Gu Hongzhong · c. 970 CE · Palace Museum, Beijing
A Southern Tang minister who suspects he is being watched throws louder and louder parties; the emperor sends Gu Hongzhong to spy, who records five scenes of the ensuing orgy. The surviving Song copy is one of the finest figural handscrolls in Chinese art.
972 CE
Islamic World
Al-Azhar Mosque
Fatimid · 972 · Cairo
The mosque and university that became the intellectual center of the Sunni world. Continuously teaching since the tenth century.
983 CE
South Asia
Gommateshwara Statue at Shravanabelagola
by Aristanemi
Ganga dynasty · 983 CE · Karnataka
A 17-meter monolithic nude of the Jain ascetic Bahubali, absorbed in meditation so deep that vines have grown up his legs. Every twelve years, pilgrims anoint the figure with milk, turmeric, and sandalwood in the Mahamastakabhisheka festival.

1000 – 1500147 works

1000
South Asia
Nataraja (Shiva as Lord of Dance)
Chola dynasty · 10th–11th c. · Tamil Nadu
Shiva dances the universe into being and out of it again, surrounded by a ring of flame, trampling the demon of ignorance.
1000
South Asia
Khajuraho Temples
Chandela dynasty · 950–1050 · Madhya Pradesh
Hindu and Jain temples covered in dense sculpture — gods, battles, and the frank erotic scenes the site is best known for.
1000
South Asia
Chola Bronzes
Chola dynasty · 9th–13th century · Tamil Nadu
Hollow-cast bronze icons of Shiva, Vishnu, and their consorts, produced by the lost-wax method for temple processions. The dynasty's patronage pushed Indian metal sculpture to its aesthetic high point — even today casters in Swamimalai work to thousand-year-old Chola templates.
1000
East Asia
Travelers Among Mountains and Streams
by Fan Kuan
Northern Song · Fan Kuan · c. 1000 CE
A seven-foot hanging scroll — a sheer cliff dominates two-thirds of the picture. Tiny travelers and mules cross the foreground, dwarfed. Widely considered the greatest Chinese landscape painting.
1000
Americas
Taos Pueblo
Tiwa · 1000+ years continuous · New Mexico
Adobe pueblos stacked five stories high. Continuously inhabited longer than any community in the U.S. — the family on the top floor climbs ladders up and down.
1000
Americas
Tumi Ceremonial Knife
Lambayeque · 900–1100 · Northern Peru
A gold sacrificial knife topped with the long-eared god Naylamp, eyes inlaid with turquoise. Used for ritual decapitation; now a symbol of Peru on souvenir shelves everywhere.
1000
Americas
El Castillo, Chichen Itzá
Maya-Toltec · c. 900–1200 CE · Yucatán
A step pyramid whose 91 steps on each of four sides plus the temple platform equal 365. On the spring and autumn equinoxes, the sun throws a serpent-shaped shadow slithering down the balustrade toward carved snake heads at the base.
1000
Americas
Tula
Toltec · c. 900–1150 CE · Hidalgo, Mexico
Capital of the Toltec — probably the 'Tollan' the Aztec considered their civilized predecessors. Colossal warrior atlantes 4.6 meters tall, bristling with atlatl spear-throwers and pectorals of stylized butterflies, still stand atop Pyramid B.
1000
Americas
Kuelap
Chachapoya · 6th–16th century · Peru
A cloud-forest citadel enclosed by twenty-meter limestone walls, sheltering over 400 round stone houses on a ridge 3,000 meters above sea level. The Chachapoya held out longer than the Inca against Spanish conquest thanks to sheer isolation.
1010
South Asia
Brihadeeswarar Temple
Chola dynasty · 1010 · Thanjavur
A granite tower 216 feet high, capped with a single 80-ton stone. A thousand years old and still in daily ritual use.
1025
South Asia
Gangaikonda Cholapuram
Chola · 1025 · Tamil Nadu
Rajendra Chola built this temple to commemorate his invasion of the Gangetic plain, bringing back a pot of Ganges water poured into the sanctum. The vimana is a softer, more feminine echo of his father's Brihadeeswarar at Thanjavur.
1026
South Asia
Modhera Sun Temple
Chaulukya dynasty · 1026 · Gujarat
Temple to Surya oriented so the first rays of the equinox sun strike the inner sanctum. A cistern called Ramakunda in front holds over a hundred miniature shrines along its steps.
1037
Europe
Saint Sophia Cathedral, Kyiv
Byzantine / Kyivan Rus · 1037 · Ukraine
A Byzantine five-nave cathedral at the heart of Kyivan Rus. Its mosaics of the Virgin Orans ("Indestructible Wall") are said to have protected the city through a thousand years of war.
1050
East Asia
Dazu Rock Carvings
Tang to Song · 9th–13th c. · Sichuan
Buddhist, Daoist, and Confucian figures carved side by side — a rare moment of syncretism preserved in stone.
1050
Americas
Atlantean Figures of Tula
Toltec · 10th–12th c. · Mexico
Fifteen-foot basalt warriors in feathered headdresses on top of Pyramid B. They held up the roof of a temple that is long gone.
1050
Americas
Chaco Canyon
Ancestral Pueblo · 850–1250 · New Mexico
A ceremonial center of "great houses" — multi-story sandstone buildings in a treeless canyon. Roads radiate out straight for sixty miles, aligned to the sky.
1050
Americas
Pueblo Bonito
Ancestral Puebloan · 9th–12th century · Chaco Canyon
A 'great house' of six hundred rooms and forty ceremonial kivas arranged in a half-D around a central plaza. Its T-shaped doors align with lunar standstills; ancient roads radiate outward across the San Juan Basin for hundreds of kilometers.
1053
East Asia
Byōdō-in
Late Heian · 1053 · Uji
The Phoenix Hall — bird-shaped pavilion reflecting in its lotus pond. Built as a noble's villa; converted to a temple when he took the tonsure.
1053
East Asia
Phoenix Hall of Byōdō-in
Heian · 1053 · Uji, Japan
Fujiwara no Yorimichi built the hall as an earthly image of Amida's Western Paradise, floating above a pond on narrow wings like a descending bird. Its central image of Amida is the only surviving work of the master sculptor Jōchō.
1070
Europe
Bayeux Tapestry
Norman · c. 1070 · France
Seventy meters of embroidered linen telling the Norman conquest of England. Probably stitched by English nuns for the new conqueror's half-brother.
1070
Islamic World
Aljafería Palace
Taifa · 11th c. · Zaragoza
A fortified palace of the Banu Hud dynasty with a surviving Islamic core — interlaced polylobed arches, stucco arabesques. Now shares a building with the regional parliament.
1070
Americas
Serpent Mound
Fort Ancient or Adena · c. 1070 CE · Ohio
A quarter-mile-long effigy mound of a snake swallowing an egg. The head aligns with sunset at the summer solstice; the coils seem to encode lunar cycles.
1072
East Asia
Early Spring
by Guo Xi
Northern Song · Guo Xi · 1072 · China
A mountain landscape in ink and subtle color. Guo Xi said a painter should look at nature "as if going on a journey" — this scroll is that walk.
1080
South Asia
Rani ki Vav
Chaulukya dynasty · 11th c. · Gujarat
A subterranean stepwell dug by Queen Udayamati for her husband. Seven levels down, 500 sculptures of Vishnu avatars and apsaras. Silted over by the Saraswati; rediscovered in the 1980s.
1102
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Shwezigon Pagoda
Pagan dynasty · 1102 · Bagan, Myanmar
A gilded cylindrical stupa that set the template for every later Burmese pagoda. King Anawrahta enshrined here the tooth of the Buddha brought from Sri Lanka — placed by an albino white elephant he released to choose the holy site.
1105
Europe
Pala d'Oro
Byzantine → Gothic goldwork · 976–1345 · Venice
The golden altarpiece of San Marco. 250 enameled panels, 1,927 gems, looted from Constantinople in 1204 and then added to across four centuries. Still in daily use.
1105
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Ananda Temple
Pagan Kingdom · 1105 · Myanmar
A cruciform white temple with four standing Buddhas, each eighteen feet tall. From a distance the gold umbrella on its stupa is the last thing to disappear.
1113
East Asia
A Thousand Li of Rivers and Mountains
by Wang Ximeng
Wang Ximeng · 1113 · Palace Museum, Beijing
An 11.9-meter Song handscroll painted in mineral blue and green by a student of eighteen who died shortly after. Hardly exhibited even in China because of the fragility of its malachite and azurite pigments.
1120
East Asia
Tale of Genji Scroll
Late Heian · early 12th c. · Japan
The oldest illustrated version of Murasaki Shikibu's Genji. The "blown-off roof" perspective looks down into private chambers; faces are reduced to lines, clothing is everything.
1136
Islamic World
Great Mosque of Tlemcen
Almoravid · 1136 · Algeria
An Almoravid foundation whose carved plaster muqarnas dome and minbar count among the most elaborate surviving examples of North African Berber craftsmanship. Its minaret was added under the Zayyanids two centuries later.
1147
Africa
Mapungubwe Hill
Mapungubwe Kingdom · 1075–1220 · South Africa
A sandstone hill-top capital that controlled Indian Ocean gold and ivory trade centuries before Great Zimbabwe. A golden rhinoceros was dug up from a royal grave here.
1150
Africa
Ethiopian Processional Cross
Ethiopian Orthodox · 12th c. onward · Ethiopia
Hand-held metal crosses carried at the front of church processions. Each is unique — geometries nested inside geometries, hammered out by a parish smith.
1150
Europe
Santiago de Compostela Cathedral
Romanesque · 1075–1211 · Galicia
The end of the Camino de Santiago — pilgrims walk 800 kilometers to this high altar and the silver tomb of St. James. The Botafumeiro swings eighty feet across the transept at mass.
1150
Islamic World
Kutubiyya Mosque
Almohad · 1150s · Marrakech
A red sandstone minaret 250 feet tall — the template for the Giralda of Seville and the Hassan Tower of Rabat. Named for the booksellers who clustered at its base.
1150
Islamic World
Alcázar of Seville
Almohad → Mudéjar → Christian · 10th–14th c.
A royal palace begun by the Abbadids, expanded by Christian kings who kept the Islamic craftsmen — the result is Mudéjar: Moorish form under Castilian rule.
1150
Islamic World
Great Mosque of Herat
Ghurid onward · 12th c. · Afghanistan
The Friday Mosque of Herat, its courtyard walls tiled in a dense cobalt and turquoise mosaic. Its tile workshops still operate beside the mosque, rebuilding what time erodes.
1150
Islamic World
Khamsa of Nizami
Persian · 12th c. poem, illuminated 14th c. onward
Five epic romances — Khosrow and Shirin, Layla and Majnun, and others — illustrated by every Persian royal workshop for centuries. The British Library copy alone has 30 miniatures.
1150
South Asia
Dilwara Temples
Jain · 11th–13th c. · Mount Abu
White marble carved so finely the columns look more like lace than stone. Every surface is covered — floor, ceiling, arch.
1150
South Asia
Gal Vihara
Polonnaruwa · 12th c. · Sri Lanka
Four colossal Buddhas carved from a single granite wall — seated, standing, reclining, meditating. The face of the reclining Buddha is nine meters long.
1150
South Asia
Jagannath Temple, Puri
Eastern Ganga · 12th c. · Odisha
Home of the annual Rath Yatra — giant wooden chariots hauled through the streets carrying the three deities. The temple's cooks feed the god daily from one of the largest kitchens in the world.
1150
South Asia
Hoysaleswara Temple
Hoysala · 1150 · Halebidu, Karnataka
Carved from chloritic schist, which hardens after quarrying, enabling the Hoysala sculptors to cut almost impossibly thin details. Elephants, musicians, and gods wrap the plinth in horizontal registers that add up to miles of relief never repeating itself.
1150
East Asia
Goryeo Celadon
Goryeo dynasty · 10th–14th c. · Korea
Stoneware with a jade-green glaze so admired that Song-dynasty Chinese called Korean celadon the "first under heaven."
1150
East Asia
Along the River During Qingming
by Zhang Zeduan
Northern Song · Zhang Zeduan · 12th c.
A five-meter handscroll showing hundreds of people in the Song capital on a spring festival day. Read right to left — it unfolds as you walk past.
1150
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Angkor Wat
Khmer Empire · 12th c. · Cambodia
The largest religious monument on earth. Built as a Hindu temple to Vishnu, converted to Buddhist use, and never fully abandoned.
1150
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Bagan
Pagan Kingdom · 11th–13th c. · Myanmar
More than 2,000 Buddhist temples and stupas on a plain by the Irrawaddy. At dawn hot air balloons drift over a landscape of brick towers.
1150
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Bayon
Khmer Empire · late 12th c. · Cambodia
The last great temple of Angkor — 200 serene faces carved into its towers, likely portraits of Jayavarman VII himself, watching from every direction.
1150
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Angkor Thom
Khmer Empire · late 12th c. · Cambodia
The last capital of the Khmer Empire — nine square kilometers walled in laterite. At its heart, the Bayon; at its gates, giants pulling serpents in cosmic tug-of-war.
1150
Americas
Dresden Codex
Late Post-Classic Maya · 11th–12th c.
One of only four surviving Maya codices — 39 sheets of bark paper folded like a screen. Astronomical tables of Venus cycles, eclipses, and the rains. Almost everything else was burned by Spanish friars.
1166
South Asia
Airavatesvara Temple
Chola · 1166 · Darasuram, Tamil Nadu
Rajaraja II's jewel-box Shiva temple, its front mandapa shaped like a chariot pulled by stone horses. The plinth is carved with musicians, dancers, and — charmingly — optical-illusion animals: a bull that doubles as an elephant depending on which end you start from.
1168
East Asia
Itsukushima Shrine
Shinto · 1168 · Miyajima, Japan
A vermilion Shinto complex on stilts in a tidal bay, with its O-torii gate standing in the water at high tide. The whole island has been considered sacred for so long that births and deaths are forbidden there.
1176
Africa
Cairo Citadel
Ayyubid → Ottoman · 1176 onward · Egypt
Saladin's fortress above old Cairo, topped centuries later by the Mosque of Muhammad Ali with its tall twin minarets — a silhouette that defines the skyline.
1180
East Asia
Chōjū-jinbutsu-giga
late Heian–Kamakura · 12th–13th century · Kōzan-ji
Four picture scrolls showing rabbits, monkeys, and frogs at human pastimes — sumo, archery, a Buddhist funeral. Often called Japan's first manga, the energetic ink line and biting satire of clergy are unparalleled in medieval East Asian art.
1185
Americas
Chan Chan
Chimú · 900–1470 CE · Peru
The largest adobe city in the Americas. Twelve miles of mud-brick walls carved with rows of seabirds, fish, and waves.
1191
Islamic World
Giralda
Almohad minaret → Christian bell tower · 1184–1198
The Almohads built it as a minaret; after the Reconquista it became a cathedral bell tower. A ramp inside let the muezzin ride up on horseback.
1195
Islamic World
Koutoubia Mosque
Almohad · 1195 · Marrakech
A seventy-seven-meter minaret whose proportions became the model for Rabat's Hassan Tower and Seville's Giralda. Its red sandstone is visible from every part of old Marrakech; Almohad law forbade taller buildings within sight.
1195
Islamic World
Hassan Tower
Almohad · 1195 · Rabat
An unfinished minaret meant to top the largest mosque in the western Islamic world — the caliph Yaqub al-Mansur died and work halted at forty-four meters, half its intended height. Rows of stumpy columns still mark where the prayer hall would have stood.
1200
Africa
Great Mosque of Kilwa
Swahili coast · 11th–14th c. · Tanzania
Coral-stone mosque on an island that dominated Indian Ocean trade. Ibn Battuta called Kilwa "one of the most beautiful cities in the world."
1200
Africa
Rock-Hewn Churches of Lalibela
Zagwe dynasty · 12th–13th c. · Ethiopia
Eleven churches carved downward into solid rock. Meant as a new Jerusalem after pilgrimage routes were cut off.
1200
Africa
Bandiagara Escarpment
Tellem and Dogon · c. 1000 CE onward · Mali
A 150-kilometer sandstone cliff that the Tellem used as burial shafts before the Dogon arrived in the fifteenth century. Granaries and shrines cling to the rockface like swallows' nests; the Dogon say their cosmology is written into the architecture.
1200
Americas
Mesa Verde Cliff Palace
Ancestral Pueblo · 12th–13th c. · Colorado
Villages tucked under sandstone overhangs. The Ancestral Puebloans built, lived in, and abandoned them in a little over a century.
1200
Americas
Cahokia Mounds
Mississippian · 1050–1350 · Illinois
The largest city in North America before Europeans arrived — bigger than London in its day. Monks Mound alone is a hundred feet tall, built of earth by hand.
1200
Americas
Chimor
Chimú · 900–1470 CE · Northern Peru
Kingdom that ruled the Peruvian coast before the Inca conquered it. Its capital Chan Chan is the largest adobe city ever built; gold, silver, and feather work survive in elite tombs.
1206
Islamic World
Qutb Minar
Delhi Sultanate · 1192–1220 · Delhi
The tallest brick minaret in the world — 238 feet of red sandstone, carved with Qur'anic calligraphy as deeply as architecture.
1207
Europe
Chartres Cathedral
High Gothic · 1194–1220 · France
Rebuilt in 26 years after a fire. Its stained glass — especially the "Chartres blue" — has never been matched; the recipe was lost.
1210
East Asia
Streams and Mountains with a Clear Distant View
by Xia Gui
Southern Song · Xia Gui · early 13th c.
A long ink handscroll in the "one-corner" style — only a fraction of the silk is painted, leaving vast voids for the viewer to read as mist and distance. A founding image of Chinese minimalism.
1220
Africa
Church of Saint George, Lalibela
Zagwe dynasty · late 12th–early 13th century · Ethiopia
Carved top-down into a single block of red volcanic tuff in the shape of a Greek cross, thirty meters on a side. King Lalibela commissioned eleven such monolithic churches after Jerusalem fell to Saladin, intending to build a new Holy Land in the Ethiopian highlands.
1237
Islamic World
Maqamat al-Hariri
by Yahya al-Wasiti
Abbasid · al-Wasiti · 1237 · Baghdad
An illustrated manuscript of Hariri's picaresque stories — streets, markets, pilgrims, drunks, courts of law. The earliest surviving paintings of daily Baghdad life.
1240
East Asia
Tripitaka Koreana
Goryeo · 1236–1251 · Haeinsa
81,258 woodblocks of the Buddhist canon carved to petition the gods to repel the Mongols. The blocks still print; stored in a 15th-century hall that manages its own humidity.
1245
Europe
Westminster Abbey
English Gothic · 1245 onward · London
Coronation church of English monarchs since 1066; also a vast, crowded graveyard — Newton, Darwin, Stephen Hawking. Poets' Corner adds Chaucer, Dickens, T. S. Eliot.
1245
Europe
Reims Cathedral
High Gothic · 1211–1275 · France
Coronation church of the kings of France. Shelled for five years in World War I; its sculptures, including the famous Smiling Angel, shattered and then were painstakingly reassembled.
1250
Africa
Great Zimbabwe
Shona · 11th–15th c. · Zimbabwe
Dry-stone walls built without mortar, enclosing a trade capital that controlled the gold road to the Indian Ocean.
1250
Africa
Great Mosque of Djenné
Mali · rebuilt 1907 (earlier mosque 13th c.)
The world's largest mud-brick building. Every spring the whole town replasters it by hand in a single day.
1250
Africa
Golden Rhinoceros of Mapungubwe
Mapungubwe · c. 1220–1290 CE · South Africa
A small wooden figurine plated with hammered gold foil, unearthed from a royal grave on Mapungubwe Hill in 1934. Apartheid-era archaeology hid the find for decades because it proved sophisticated African statehood before European contact.
1250
Europe
Cologne Cathedral
High Gothic · 1248–1880 · Germany
Started in 1248, stopped in 1473, resumed in 1842, finished with the original medieval drawings in hand. For four years after completion it was the tallest building on earth.
1250
Europe
Siena Cathedral
Italian Gothic · 1196–1348
Striped in black and white marble. The floor is inlaid with 56 narrative panels — the largest program of marble intarsia in Italy, covered most of the year to protect it.
1250
South Asia
Konark Sun Temple
Eastern Ganga dynasty · 13th c. · Odisha
A massive stone chariot for the sun god, pulled by seven horses, wheels carved to tell the time from their shadows.
1252
East Asia
Great Buddha of Kamakura
Kamakura period · 1252 · Japan
A bronze Amida Buddha originally housed in a great hall — but tsunamis washed the building away in 1498 and left the statue to sit in the open ever since.
1254
Europe
Notre-Dame de Paris
Gothic · 1163–1345 · Paris
Cathedral of the kings of France. Half the fabric is original medieval, half is Viollet-le-Duc's 19th-century reimagining. The 2019 fire took the spire.
1266
East Asia
Sanjūsangen-dō
Kamakura period · 1266 · Kyoto
A 390-foot hall holding 1,001 gilded statues of the thousand-armed Kannon. Each face is different; pilgrims look for their own.
1268
South Asia
Chennakeshava Temple, Somanathapura
Hoysala · 1268 · Karnataka
A star-plan temple with three shrines on a single platform, surrounded by a cloistered courtyard. Every surface is carved: gods in scalloped niches, musicians in frothing bands, a frieze of marching elephants at ground level supporting the whole weight of the building.
1280
Europe
Rose Window
Gothic · 13th c. · Strasbourg
A massive tracery wheel of stained glass — the Gothic cathedral's visual sermon, laying out salvation in colored light. Chartres' rose blue was never surpassed; Strasbourg's west rose is a rival.
1280
East Asia
Samguk Yusa
Goryeo · 13th c. · Korea
"Memorabilia of the Three Kingdoms" — a monk's compilation of legends and history. The main surviving source for Korea's founding myths and for early Korean Buddhism.
1280
East Asia
Heiji Monogatari Emaki
Kamakura · late 13th century · MFA Boston and Tokyo National Museum
Handscrolls illustrating the 1159 Heiji Disturbance, a coup attempt that left much of Kyoto burning. The burning-palace scroll unrolls into perhaps the greatest battle scene in Japanese art, smoke curling above warriors climbing a wheeled carriage.
1300
Africa
Bronze Head from Ife
Yoruba · 12th–15th c. · Nigeria
Naturalistic portraits cast in brass with lost-wax technique. When first seen in Europe they were disbelieved as African work.
1300
Africa
Sankore Madrasa
Mali/Songhai · c. 989 onward · Timbuktu
An earthen mosque-university whose pyramidal minaret's dimensions match the Kaaba in Mecca. Its scholars curated one of the largest libraries in medieval Africa; tens of thousands of manuscripts from Sankore survive, hidden in family chests when Tuareg raiders closed in.
1300
Europe
Florence Cathedral (Duomo)
Italian Gothic / Renaissance · 1296–1436
Brunelleschi's dome, still the largest brick dome ever built. He designed it without drawings, built it without scaffolding, and took the method with him to the grave.
1300
Islamic World
Alhambra
Nasrid dynasty · 13th–14th c. · Granada, Spain
A palace-citadel of courtyards, fountains, and muqarnas ceilings. The poems inscribed on the walls are part of the architecture.
1300
East Asia
Shigaraki Ware
Muromachi onward · 13th c. · Shiga
Earthy brown stoneware with feldspar eruptions on the surface. Prized for tea — the accidental "fire colors" and ash deposits are the whole point.
1300
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Sukhothai Historical Park
Sukhothai Kingdom · 13th–14th century · Thailand
Capital of the first Thai kingdom, whose 'walking Buddhas' introduced a uniquely flowing and androgynous figural type. The open ruins of Wat Si Chum still frame a 15-meter seated Buddha peering out from a narrow gap between high walls.
1300
Americas
Paquimé (Casas Grandes)
Mogollon · 13th–14th c. · Chihuahua, Mexico
Multi-story adobe pueblos north of the Maya world but connected to them — Paquimé traded in parrots, copper bells, and turquoise. Burned and abandoned before the Spanish arrived.
1300
Americas
Muisca Raft (El Dorado)
Muisca · 600–1600 CE · Colombia
A miniature gold raft showing the chief and his attendants during the initiation ritual in Lake Guatavita — the story that grew into the European myth of El Dorado.
1300
Americas
Tulum
Late Post-classic Maya · c. 1200–1500 CE · Yucatán
A small Maya port perched on twelve-meter cliffs above the Caribbean, walled on three landward sides. The Temple of the Descending God shows an inverted deity plunging head-first — probably the bee god so central to Yucatán cosmology.
1300
Americas
Moundville
Mississippian · 1000–1450 CE · Alabama
A 300-acre ceremonial center with twenty-nine earthen platform mounds around a plaza, once home to about a thousand people. Moundville engravers produced copper plates of the Birdman and hand-and-eye motifs that defined the Southeastern Ceremonial Complex.
1304
Europe
Scrovegni Chapel Frescoes
by Giotto di Bondone
Proto-Renaissance · 1303–1305 · Padua
Giotto's cycle of the life of the Virgin and Christ. A man paid for it to save his father from hell — his father appears in the painting, handing over the chapel model.
1307
Islamic World
Dome of Soltaniyeh
Ilkhanid Mongol · 1302–1312 · Iran
Second only to Hagia Sophia's in size when built. A blue-tiled double-shell dome soaring over the tomb of the Ilkhanid ruler Oljeitu — centuries ahead of its engineering.
1325
Islamic World
Al-Attarine Madrasa
Marinid · 1325 · Fez
A tiny college next to the spice market whose four walls are totally covered in carved plaster, cedar, and zellij. Every square inch repays close looking: calligraphic friezes in Kufic and Thuluth, muqarnas capitals, and a delicately fountained courtyard only seven meters across.
1327
Africa
Djinguereber Mosque
Mali Empire · 1327 · Timbuktu
Commissioned by Mansa Musa after his pilgrimage to Mecca. An Andalusian architect built it in earth and fossil limestone — one of three medieval mosques still standing in Timbuktu.
1330
Islamic World
Baptistery of Saint Louis
c. 1320–1340 · Hammered brass inlaid with silver and gold · Cour Visconti
A Mamluk basin made in Egypt or Syria, covered in inlaid scenes of hunters, courtiers, and animals. At some point it entered the French royal treasury, and by tradition — probably invented — it was used for the baptism of royal children, including the future Louis XIII.
1350
Africa
Dogon Cliff Villages
Dogon · 14th c. onward · Mali
Mud granaries and shrines stacked along the Bandiagara Escarpment. The Dogon moved here fleeing forced conversion and stayed half a millennium.
1350
Islamic World
Po-i-Kalyan
Timurid · 12th–16th c. · Bukhara
A minaret, mosque, and madrasa across one square. Tradition says Genghis Khan spared the minaret because his hat fell off as he looked up at it.
1350
Islamic World
Shah Cheragh
Qajar · 14th c. onward · Shiraz
A Shia shrine covered inside floor to ceiling in thousands of shards of mirror. Candlelight becomes a galaxy. Pilgrims hold a finger against the wall and watch their reflection splinter.
1350
Islamic World
Bou Inania Madrasa
Marinid · 1350–1355 · Fez
A teaching mosque of carved cedar, zellige tile, and stucco so fine it looks punched through paper. The Marinid sultan reportedly said the building cost was so beautiful it didn't matter what it cost.
1350
East Asia
Dwelling in the Fuchun Mountains
by Huang Gongwang
Huang Gongwang · 1350 · split between Taipei and Hangzhou
A Yuan-dynasty masterpiece painted for a Daoist friend, its 6.9-meter scroll burned in two in 1650 by a distraught collector trying to take it with him to the afterlife. The two surviving halves were reunited for one exhibition in 2011 after 360 years apart.
1350
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Sukhothai
Sukhothai Kingdom · 13th–15th c. · Thailand
Serene standing Buddhas in the "walking" pose invented here — one hand raised, the other swinging at the hip, fabric flowing forward.
1350
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Besakih Temple
Balinese Hindu · 14th c. onward · Bali
The Mother Temple of Bali — 23 linked sanctuaries up the slope of Mount Agung. When Agung erupted in 1963, lava stopped meters from its gates.
1359
Islamic World
Mosque-Madrasa of Sultan Hasan
Mamluk · 1356–1363 · Cairo
A monumental stone box with a towering cruciform courtyard. One of the most audacious pieces of medieval Islamic architecture, by an unknown hand.
1375
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Moai
Rapa Nui · 1250–1500 · Easter Island
Nearly 900 stone figures, carved and moved miles from the quarry. They face inland — watching over the villages, not out to sea.
1383
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Wat Phra That Doi Suthep
Lanna · 1383 · Chiang Mai
A mountaintop temple reached by a staircase of 306 steps flanked by mosaic-tiled nagas. Legend says King Nu Naone chose the site by tying a Buddha relic to a white elephant and following where it fell; it collapsed here and died.
1395
East Asia
Gyeongbokgung
Joseon dynasty · 1395 · Seoul
The principal palace of the Joseon kings. Burned by the Japanese in 1592, rebuilt in 1867, mostly destroyed again under colonial rule — now being re-reconstructed, wing by wing.
1395
East Asia
Jongmyo Shrine
Joseon · 1395 · Seoul
The Confucian royal ancestral shrine of the Joseon dynasty, a 101-meter wooden hall whose austere horizontal roofline is the longest of its kind in East Asia. Twice-yearly rites performed here preserve court music banned in revolutionary China and Vietnam.
1397
East Asia
Kinkaku-ji (Golden Pavilion)
Muromachi · 1397, rebuilt 1955 · Kyoto
A retirement villa turned Zen temple, its top two floors wrapped in gold leaf. Burned down by a young monk in 1950 — rebuilt in facsimile.
1400
Islamic World
Mausoleum of Khoja Ahmed Yasawi
Timurid · 1389–1405 · Turkistan, Kazakhstan
Commissioned by Timur for a Sufi poet's tomb. Left unfinished when Timur died; the scaffolding on the portal front is six centuries old.
1400
South Asia
Thangka Painting
Tibetan Buddhist · c. 11th century onward · Himalayan region
Scroll paintings on cotton or silk, bordered with brocade, depicting deities in complex mandalic geometries for visualization practice. Pigments are ground from powdered turquoise, cinnabar, and malachite — gold highlights are burnished with a dog's tooth.
1400
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Nan Madol
Saudeleur dynasty · 12th–17th c. · Pohnpei
A city of 92 artificial islets built from hexagonal basalt logs, laid across a lagoon. The "Venice of the Pacific" — and a place no one has fully explained.
1400
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Ahu and Moai of Easter Island
Rapa Nui · c. 1250–1500 CE · Rapa Nui
Ceremonial stone platforms on which the moai stood, facing inland over the village. A typical ahu runs 100 meters along the coast; some incorporate basalt pillars and red scoria pukao 'topknots' rolled from a separate quarry.
1400
Americas
Mitla
Late Zapotec · 1200–1500 CE · Oaxaca
Religious capital of the Zapotec after the fall of Monte Albán, whose façades are veneered in thousands of perfectly fitted stone mosaic pieces forming grecas — geometric frets without a single repeating unit in some panels.
1401
Islamic World
Bibi-Khanym Mosque
Timurid · 1399–1404 · Samarkand
Timur commissioned it after sacking Delhi — the largest mosque in the Islamic world at the time. The main dome collapsed within decades; centuries later the Soviets rebuilt it.
1403
Islamic World
Gur-e Amir
Timurid · 1403 · Samarkand
The tomb of Timur under a ribbed turquoise dome. The grave was opened in 1941 with a curse; Germany invaded the USSR three days later.
1413
East Asia
Forbidden City
Ming & Qing · 1406–1420 onward · Beijing
980 buildings on 180 acres. For five centuries, commoners who entered uninvited were executed. It is now the world's most-visited museum.
1420
East Asia
Temple of Heaven
Ming · 1420 · Beijing
A round blue-roofed temple on a square base — heaven over earth. Every winter solstice for five centuries, the emperor prayed here for the harvest.
1432
Europe
Ghent Altarpiece
by Jan van Eyck
Early Netherlandish · Jan van Eyck · 1432 · Belgium
Twelve folding panels of unmatched detail. Stolen seven times across its history; the Just Judges panel has been missing since 1934.
1434
Europe
The Arnolfini Portrait
by Jan van Eyck
Early Netherlandish · Jan van Eyck · 1434
A merchant and his wife in a Bruges interior. The convex mirror on the back wall reflects two more figures — one of them possibly the painter.
1450
Africa
Larabanga Mosque
Sudano-Sahelian · 15th c. · Ghana
A mud-and-stick mosque painted white, bristling with wooden supports. The oldest mosque in West Africa still in its original form.
1450
Islamic World
Shah-i-Zinda
Timurid · 11th–19th c. · Samarkand
A necropolis of tiled mausoleums along a single lane. The tomb complex for the cousin of the Prophet — and for every Timurid noble who wanted to lie near him.
1450
South Asia
Hampi (Vijayanagara)
Vijayanagara Empire · 14th–16th c. · Karnataka
Capital of the last great Hindu empire in the south. Sacked in 1565; the boulder-strewn ruins sprawl over 16 square miles.
1450
South Asia
Ranakpur Jain Temple
Jain · 15th c. · Rajasthan
1,444 carved marble columns, no two alike. The complex is aligned so that the sun reaches the central sanctum for only a few days a year.
1450
East Asia
Ryōan-ji Rock Garden
Muromachi · late 15th c. · Kyoto
Fifteen stones in raked gravel. From no single vantage can you see all fifteen at once — you're always one short of a complete view.
1450
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Candi Sukuh
Majapahit · 15th c. · Java
A Hindu temple built as a truncated stone pyramid — closer to Maya than to Angkor. Its reliefs are frank about fertility; the statues at the gate tell you what they're for.
1450
Americas
Coatlicue
Mexica · late 15th c. · Tenochtitlan
"She of the Serpent Skirt" — the earth goddess, severed neck bleeding two snakes, wearing a necklace of hands and hearts. Found under Mexico City in 1790.
1450
Americas
Aztec Sun Stone
Mexica · late 15th c. · Tenochtitlan
A basalt disk over three meters across, carving the five successive suns of Aztec cosmology. Buried under the Zócalo in Mexico City until 1790.
1450
Americas
Sacsayhuamán
Inca · 15th c. · Cusco, Peru
A fortress-temple above Cusco built from stones weighing up to 200 tons, fitted so precisely a sheet of paper won't pass between them.
1450
Americas
Machu Picchu
Inca · 15th c. · Peru
A royal estate on a knife-edged ridge. The stones of its walls are cut so precisely that no mortar holds them — and they have survived five centuries of earthquakes.
1450
Americas
Coyolxāuhqui Stone
Mexica · late 15th c. · Tenochtitlan
A twelve-foot disk of the moon goddess, dismembered by her brother Huitzilopochtli. Found by electric-company workers in Mexico City in 1978 — it launched modern excavation of the Templo Mayor.
1450
Americas
Ollantaytambo
Inca · 15th c. · Sacred Valley
Fortress and town at the end of the Sacred Valley. Its huge terrace walls are fitted together in the same mortarless style as Machu Picchu — and in 1537 its defenders held off Pizarro's cavalry from them.
1450
Americas
Coricancha
Inca · c. 1200–1500 · Cuzco
The Inca 'Golden Enclosure,' a Sun temple whose walls were once sheathed in gold plate stripped by the Spanish for ransom. The surviving trapezoidal masonry — so precise the joints cannot be penetrated by a knife blade — now supports the colonial Church of Santo Domingo.
1458
Islamic World
Eyüp Sultan Mosque
Ottoman · 1458 (rebuilt 1800) · Istanbul
Built where the companion of Muhammad, Abu Ayyub al-Ansari, was said to have died during the 7th-century Arab siege of Constantinople. Ottoman sultans were girded with the sword of Osman at his tomb; the courtyard is still a pilgrimage site across the Sunni world.
1460
South Asia
Mehrangarh Fort
Rathore dynasty · 1459 onward · Jodhpur
A hundred-meter cliff-top fort in the Blue City of Jodhpur. Its walls still bear handprints of the widows of maharajas who threw themselves on the pyre in the medieval practice of sati.
1472
Islamic World
Tiled Kiosk
Ottoman · 1472 · Topkapı, Istanbul
Mehmed II's pavilion inside the first courtyard of Topkapı, used for watching polo matches. Its façade of star-and-hexagon tiles in cuerda seca and its blue-and-white mosaic entrance are direct echoes of Persian and Central Asian craft recruited to the new Ottoman capital.
1480
Europe
Primavera
by Sandro Botticelli
Early Renaissance · Botticelli · c. 1480 · Florence
Venus in a grove of citrus. The three Graces, Mercury, and the story of Chloris turning into Flora — a Medici wedding gift, decoded differently in every generation.
1482
East Asia
Ginkaku-ji (Silver Pavilion)
Muromachi · 1482 · Kyoto
Ashikaga Yoshimasa's retirement villa, intended but never actually clad in silver foil. Its ascetic gray-wood restraint and the adjacent sand garden — Moon-Viewing Platform and Sea of Silver Sand — crystallized the Higashiyama aesthetic of Japanese tea culture.
1485
Europe
The Birth of Venus
by Sandro Botticelli
Early Renaissance · Botticelli · c. 1485 · Florence
Venus rises from the sea on a shell, blown to shore by wind-gods. Painted on canvas when fresco was still the fashion — and kept hidden from Savonarola's fires.
1487
Americas
Templo Mayor
Aztec · 1325–1521 · Tenochtitlan
Dual pyramid of Huitzilopochtli and Tlaloc, discovered beneath downtown Mexico City in 1978 when utility workers struck the Coyolxauhqui Stone. Seven construction phases climb one over the other inside the exposed ruin.
1493
Islamic World
Tomb of Bibi Jawindi
Delhi Sultanate · 1493 · Uch Sharif, Pakistan
An octagonal blue-tiled tomb whose eastern half collapsed in a nineteenth-century flood. What survives — Multani-style glazed brickwork in lapis, turquoise, and white on red — is among the finest examples of pre-Mughal funerary architecture in the subcontinent.
1495
Africa
Tomb of Askia
Songhai Empire · 1495 · Gao, Mali
A seventeen-meter pyramidal mud tower bristling with protruding palm-wood timbers, built for emperor Askia Mohammed after his pilgrimage to Mecca. The timbers double as permanent scaffolding for the annual replastering of its earthen skin.
1495
Europe
Drawings by Leonardo da Vinci
by Leonardo da Vinci
c. 1480–1510 · Chalk, ink, and silverpoint on paper
The Louvre holds over twenty sheets by Leonardo — studies of hands, horses, the tilt of a woman's head, the flow of water. A single silverpoint line, set down and never corrected.
1496
Europe
The Last Supper
by Leonardo da Vinci
High Renaissance · Leonardo · 1495–1498 · Milan
Painted on a refectory wall in an experimental technique that began flaking within decades. The fragility is part of the legend.
1498
Europe
Pietà
by Michelangelo
High Renaissance · Michelangelo · 1498–1499 · Vatican
Carved by a 24-year-old. Mary is impossibly young — younger than her son — because, Michelangelo said, virtue preserves her. His only signed work.

1500 – 1800146 works

1500
Africa
Great Mosque of Kano
Hausa · 15th c. onward · Nigeria
A mud-and-stick Friday mosque rebuilt many times across six centuries. The current building rose after a 1937 fire; minarets slender as reeds above a walled city.
1500
Africa
Swahili Stone Towns
Swahili coast · 12th–19th c. · East Africa
Coral-stone trading towns — Lamu, Pate, Kilwa — with carved wooden doors studded in brass, Indian Ocean dhows in their harbors, and mosques whose minarets face inland.
1500
Africa
Khami Ruins
Torwa state · c. 1450–1650 · Zimbabwe
A stone city of terraced platforms retained by decorative chevron and checker patterns in dry-laid granite. Successor to Great Zimbabwe, Khami traded Ming porcelain and Portuguese beads with the east African coast before being burned in the seventeenth century.
1500
Europe
The Garden of Earthly Delights
by Hieronymus Bosch
Early Netherlandish · Bosch · c. 1500 · Spain
A triptych of Eden, excess, and hell. The right panel is crowded with monsters nobody has fully decoded — including one with sheet music tattooed on a buttock.
1500
South Asia
Boudhanath Stupa
Newar/Tibetan Buddhist · 15th century · Kathmandu
One of the largest stupas in Asia, thirty-six meters across, its dome topped by the painted eyes of the Buddha watching in four directions. Circumambulated daily by Tibetan refugees, pilgrims, and locals — prayer-wheel grooves are worn smooth into the granite.
1500
East Asia
Great Wall of China
Ming (current form) · 14th–17th c.
Not one wall but many, rebuilt across dynasties. The Ming sections visitors see today are a fraction of a system that once stretched over 13,000 miles.
1500
East Asia
Buncheong Ware
Early Joseon · 15th–16th century · Korea
Stoneware coated in a pale slip and then incised, stamped, or brushed with freely drawn fish, birds, and peonies. Its looseness horrified the Chinese-influenced court but enchanted Japanese tea masters, who smuggled kilns full of it home in the 1590s.
1500
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Polynesian Tiki
Polynesian · pre-contact · Pacific
Stone or wooden carvings of deified ancestors, dotting the Marquesas, Tahiti, and Hawaii. Variants spread as far as Easter Island's moai.
1500
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Marae
Polynesian · pre-European contact · central and eastern Pacific
Open-air rectangular ceremonial courtyards paved in coral rubble, with upright stone backrests for chiefs and priests. From Tahiti to Hawaii to Aotearoa, the marae was the meeting ground of cosmology, law, and genealogical recitation.
1500
Americas
Hiawatha Belt
Haudenosaunee · c. 1500 · Northeast Woodlands
A wampum belt recording the founding of the Iroquois Confederacy: five nations joined by a central tree. Belts were both treaty and record — texts read by running fingers across the beads.
1502
Europe
David
by Michelangelo
High Renaissance · Michelangelo · 1501–1504
Seventeen feet of marble carved from a block others had given up on. David faces Goliath before the stone flies — tense, not triumphant.
1510
Europe
The School of Athens
by Raphael
High Renaissance · Raphael · 1509–1511
Plato and Aristotle at the center, surrounded by the Greek philosophical tradition. Raphael slipped portraits of himself and Michelangelo into the crowd.
1510
Europe
Sistine Chapel Ceiling
by Michelangelo
High Renaissance · Michelangelo · 1508–1512
Four years, mostly alone, on scaffolding. Michelangelo considered himself a sculptor, not a painter, and complained about the job the whole way through.
1510
Europe
Mona Lisa (La Joconde)
by Leonardo da Vinci
c. 1503–1519 · Oil on poplar · Salle des États
Probably a portrait of Lisa Gherardini, wife of a Florentine merchant. Leonardo carried it with him for years, reworking it endlessly, and it never left his side until his death in France. Her fame owes as much to theft as to genius: when Vincenzo Peruggia stole her in 1911 and kept her in a Paris apartment for two years, the empty wall drew larger crowds than the painting ever had.
1510
South Asia
Virupaksha Temple, Hampi
Vijayanagara · 7th century onward · Karnataka
The only temple in Hampi still in continuous worship after the sack of 1565. Its nine-tiered gopura pours down layers of painted deities; in the courtyard a pinhole in the outer wall projects the gopura inverted onto the opposite wall — a Vijayanagara camera obscura.
1513
Europe
Knight, Death and the Devil
by Albrecht Dürer
1513 · Engraving
One of Dürer's three "Master Engravings." A Christian knight rides impassively through a desolate gorge, unmoved by the hourglass-bearing Death beside him or the goat-headed Devil behind. The technical summit of Renaissance printmaking.
1513
Europe
Sistine Madonna
by Raphael
High Renaissance · 1512–1513 · Dresden
Mary descending on clouds; two bored cherubs resting their chins on the painting's lower frame. Those cherubs — cropped, enlarged, spun off — became one of the most reproduced images of any era.
1514
Europe
The Isenheim Altarpiece
by Matthias Grünewald
Northern Renaissance · Grünewald · 1512–1516 · Alsace
Painted for a hospital for plague and ergotism. Christ's body on the cross is covered in exactly the sores the patients saw on their own.
1514
Europe
Melencolia I
by Albrecht Dürer
Northern Renaissance · 1514
A winged figure sits surrounded by tools — compass, scales, hourglass — unable to act. Dürer's engraving of creative despair, loaded with symbols art historians still argue about.
1514
Europe
The Dying Slave
by Michelangelo
c. 1513–1515 · Marble · Denon Wing
Intended for the tomb of Pope Julius II, a project that consumed Michelangelo for forty years and was never finished as planned. The figure is not dying but — perhaps — falling asleep, or surrendering to ecstasy. Michelangelo left the back deliberately rough.
1530
East Asia
Qiu Ying Paintings
by Qiu Ying
Ming · Qiu Ying · 1494–1552
One of the "Four Masters of the Ming Dynasty." Rose from lacquer-worker to court painter. His handscrolls of palace scenes and mountain recluses are among the most copied in Chinese art history.
1533
Europe
The Ambassadors
by Hans Holbein the Younger
Northern Renaissance · 1533
Two French diplomats stand beside a table of instruments — lutes, globes, sundials. At the bottom, a distorted smear that becomes a human skull when you look from the side. A memento mori in code.
1539
Islamic World
Ardabil Carpet
Safavid Persia · 1539–1540 · Iran
Medallion carpet with 25 million knots. Made as a pair — the other half sold off piece by piece to repair this one. Now in the V&A.
1540
South Asia
Lepakshi Temple
Vijayanagara · 16th c. · Andhra Pradesh
A granite temple to Shiva whose ceiling holds the largest surviving Vijayanagara mural. One of its columns hangs a fraction of an inch above the floor — pilgrims pass cloth beneath it.
1542
Americas
Codex Mendoza
Early colonial Mexican · 1541–1542
Aztec tribute lists and imperial history commissioned for Charles V. Captured by French pirates en route to Spain, ended up in Oxford's Bodleian. The most detailed document we have of Aztec administration.
1548
Islamic World
Şehzade Mosque
by Mimar Sinan
Ottoman · 1548 · Istanbul
Sinan's first imperial commission, built for Suleiman the Magnificent in memory of his eldest son Şehzade Mehmed. The architect later dismissed it as an apprentice work — in fact it established the centralized four-semi-dome plan he would perfect at Selimiye.
1550
Africa
Bronze Head of Queen Idia
Benin kingdom · 16th c. · Nigeria
Portrait of the first Queen Mother of Benin, cast to sit on an ancestral altar. One of the most famous of the Benin bronzes.
1550
Africa
Queen Mother Pendant Mask (Iyoba)
Edo kingdom · 16th c. · Nigeria
Ivory mask worn on the belt of the Oba of Benin. The forehead tiaras are miniature Portuguese heads — trading partners carved as ornament.
1550
Africa
Harar Jugol
Walled old city · 16th century · Ethiopia
Called Islam's fourth holy city, with 82 mosques and 102 shrines packed inside its five-gated wall. Harari houses are painted bright interior hues and display hung baskets — the craft tradition of married women signaling wealth and taste.
1550
Islamic World
Registan of Samarkand
Timurid · 15th–17th c. · Uzbekistan
Three madrasas framing a public square — Ulugh Beg's was home to one of the greatest astronomical observatories of the medieval world.
1550
Islamic World
İznik Pottery
Ottoman · 15th–17th c. · Anatolia
The workshop town that supplied tiles for mosques from Istanbul to Damascus. The signature tomato-red slip was a short-lived chemical triumph.
1550
Islamic World
Shahnameh of Shah Tahmasp
Safavid · 16th c. · Iran
A royal copy of Ferdowsi's epic with 258 miniatures painted by the court workshop — mountains with gold streams, demons in the rocks. One of the great illuminated manuscripts.
1550
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Ayutthaya
Ayutthaya Kingdom · 14th–18th c. · Thailand
The Thai capital before the Burmese sacked it in 1767. Brick prangs lean at odd angles around the old moated island.
1550
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Pura Tanah Lot
Balinese Hindu · 16th c. · Bali
A Hindu sea temple perched on a rock pounded by surf. At high tide it becomes an island; legend says sea snakes guard it from the base.
1553
Islamic World
Süleymaniye Mosque
Ottoman · Sinan · 1550–1557 · Istanbul
Mimar Sinan's masterpiece from the middle of his career — the architect's own tomb is in the garden outside.
1560
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Wat Xieng Thong
Lan Xang · 1559–1560 · Luang Prabang
The royal temple of the Lao kingdom. Its sim has a sweeping tiered roof that nearly touches the ground; interior walls are covered in gilded-black lacquer tell-tales.
1563
Europe
The Wedding at Cana
by Paolo Veronese
Venetian Renaissance · 1563 · Louvre, Paris
The largest painting in the Louvre at seventy square meters. Painted for a Venetian refectory; Napoleon's troops cut it in half in 1797 to ship it to France.
1563
Islamic World
Rüstem Pasha Mosque
by Mimar Sinan
Ottoman · 1563 · Istanbul
Sinan designed this compact mosque for the grand vizier Rüstem Pasha above a row of shops, hiding the prayer hall on a raised platform. It is clad inside and out in the most lavish İznik tiles Sinan ever deployed — flowers bloom on every surface.
1565
Europe
Hunters in the Snow
by Pieter Bruegel the Elder
Northern Renaissance · 1565
Three hunters returning empty-handed to a frozen village; dogs slink behind them; skaters on the far ponds. One of the most lived-in winter scenes ever painted.
1565
Islamic World
Ben Youssef Madrasa
Saadian · 1565 · Marrakech
Once the largest Quranic college in North Africa, housing up to 900 students in tiny cells around a central courtyard. Cedar, marble, and zellij tilework interlock in concentric geometric schemes calibrated for the hours of daylight prayer.
1570
Islamic World
Agra Fort
Mughal · 1565–1573 · Uttar Pradesh
Red sandstone fort rebuilt by Akbar, later inner palaces added by Shah Jahan. Imprisoned here by his own son Aurangzeb, Shah Jahan could see the Taj Mahal across the river until his death.
1570
South Asia
Humayun's Tomb
Mughal · 1570 · Delhi
The first garden tomb of the Mughals and the prototype for the Taj Mahal eighty years later. A red sandstone dome in a four-quartered paradise garden.
1571
South Asia
Fatehpur Sikri
Mughal · Akbar · 1571 · Uttar Pradesh
A red sandstone capital built and abandoned in fifteen years when the water ran out. The ghost city of the greatest of the Mughals.
1573
Islamic World
Sidi Saiyyed Mosque Screens
Gujarat Sultanate · 1573 · Ahmedabad
Ten pierced-stone jali windows of the tree-of-life pattern — a tamarind tree winding through palm fronds — rank among the most reproduced motifs of Indo-Islamic art. The Indian Institute of Management Ahmedabad adopted it as a logo.
1575
Islamic World
Selimiye Mosque
Ottoman · Sinan · 1575 · Edirne
Sinan called it the masterpiece of his old age. Eight piers lift a dome wider than Hagia Sophia's — the height of Ottoman engineering.
1578
Islamic World
Saadian Tombs
Saadian · 1578 · Marrakech
Lost behind a high wall after the Alaouite sultan Moulay Ismail sealed their entrance, then rediscovered from aerial photos in 1917. The Hall of Twelve Columns — gilded muqarnas vaults over sixty-six princely graves in white Carrara marble — is among the finest interiors in Morocco.
1590
East Asia
Cypress Trees
by Kanō Eitoku
Momoyama Japan · Kanō Eitoku · 1590
An eight-panel folding screen with a single enormous cypress sprawling across gold leaf. The Kano school's monumental court style at its peak.
1591
East Asia
Nishi Hongan-ji
Jōdo Shinshū · 1591 · Kyoto
Head temple of the Jōdo Shinshū Buddhist sect. The Momoyama-period audience hall Shiroshoin is decorated with lavish painted wooden ceilings and gold-leaf panels commissioned by Toyotomi Hideyoshi — among the most opulent surviving interiors of the age.
1596
Europe
Bacchus
by Caravaggio
Baroque · c. 1596 · Florence
Caravaggio's boyish, tipsy Bacchus — fingernails dirty, a decaying fruit basket in the foreground. Lost for three centuries, rediscovered in a Uffizi storeroom in 1913.
1598
Islamic World
Naqsh-e Jahan Square
Safavid · 1598 onward · Isfahan
One of the largest squares in the world. Built by Shah Abbas to stage polo, military reviews, and the stateliness of a capital — framed on four sides by a mosque, a palace, a bazaar, and a private chapel.
1599
Europe
The Calling of Saint Matthew
by Caravaggio
Baroque · 1599–1600 · Rome
Christ points across a shadowed tavern. A shaft of light slides down the back wall. Matthew gestures at himself — "me?" — caravaggio inventing a whole new visual language.
1599
Europe
Judith Beheading Holofernes
by Caravaggio
Baroque · c. 1599
Judith, arms extended to keep the blood off her dress, saws through the Assyrian general's neck. Caravaggio's first great biblical violence — the beginning of his reputation.
1600
Africa
Benin Bronzes
Edo kingdom · 16th–17th c. · Nigeria
Plaques that once covered pillars of the royal palace in Benin City. Looted in 1897; still being repatriated today.
1600
Europe
St. Peter's Basilica
Renaissance &amp; Baroque · 1506–1626 · Vatican
Built over the tomb of Peter. Bramante started it, Michelangelo finished the dome, Bernini added the piazza's embracing colonnade — the collective monument of Renaissance Rome.
1600
Islamic World
Ali Qapu
Safavid · early 17th c. · Isfahan
Six-story royal pavilion overlooking Naqsh-e Jahan Square. Its upper music room has plaster cut into silhouettes of jars and vases — both ornament and acoustic resonator.
1600
South Asia
Meenakshi Temple
Nayak period · 16th–17th c. · Madurai
Fourteen gateway towers covered in thousands of painted figures. The whole complex is repainted every twelve years.
1600
East Asia
Blue and White Porcelain
Yuan → Qing · 14th–19th c. · Jingdezhen
Cobalt under a clear glaze, fired to translucence. China's most-exported art form — copied in Iran, Turkey, Netherlands, and eventually the world.
1600
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Tongkonan
Toraja · centuries old · Sulawesi, Indonesia
Ancestral houses with boat-shaped roofs soaring up at both ends. Each is built facing north, aligned to the cosmos of the Torajan dead.
1609
South Asia
Akbar's Tomb
Mughal · 1605–1613 · Sikandra, Agra
A five-story mausoleum on a platform in a paradise garden. Akbar designed much of it himself; Jahangir's later changes produced the strange tiered result.
1609
East Asia
Himeji Castle
Edo period · 1609 · Hyōgo, Japan
The finest surviving Japanese castle, nicknamed "White Heron" for its white plaster. Its winding approach was designed to confuse besiegers.
1610
Europe
Samson and Delilah (Rubens)
by Peter Paul Rubens
Peter Paul Rubens · 1610 · National Gallery, London
Delilah watches tenderly while a Philistine crops Samson's hair as he sleeps on her lap. The candlelit nocturne and coiled composition mark Rubens's first mature statement after returning from Italy, still vibrating with Caravaggio.
1612
Islamic World
Blue Mosque (Sultan Ahmed)
Ottoman · 1609–1616 · Istanbul
The Ottoman answer to Hagia Sophia across the square. More than 20,000 İznik tiles line the interior — the blue that gave it its Western name.
1615
Islamic World
Sheikh Lotfollah Mosque
Safavid · 1603–1619 · Isfahan
The private royal chapel on the east side of Naqsh-e Jahan Square. No minarets, no courtyard — just a single domed hall whose tilework, buff and turquoise, changes color with the sun.
1615
East Asia
Mt. Fuji Tea Bowl
by Hon'ami Kōetsu
Edo Japan · Hon'ami Kōetsu · early 17th c.
A Raku-ware tea bowl with a white glazed rim called Fujisan — Mount Fuji. Kōetsu was a calligrapher, lacquer master, and sword-polisher as well. A bowl meant to be cradled.
1617
East Asia
Nikkō Tōshō-gū
Early Edo · 1617 · Japan
The mausoleum of Tokugawa Ieyasu, built with the lavish polychrome carving the Tokugawa banned elsewhere. The three wise monkeys are here, above a stable.
1620
Islamic World
Shah Mosque, Isfahan
Safavid Persia · 1611–1629 · Iran
Seven-colour tilework on the scale of a whole square. Inside the dome, one clap echoes seven times.
1620
East Asia
Katsura Imperial Villa
Edo Japan · 1620 onward · Kyoto
A villa and garden considered one of the finest masterpieces of Japanese architecture. Shoin-zukuri precision: every plank, every sight-line from every veranda, deliberately aligned.
1622
Europe
The Rape of Proserpina
by Gian Lorenzo Bernini
Gian Lorenzo Bernini · 1622 · Galleria Borghese
Pluto's fingertips sink visibly into Proserpina's thigh — marble impersonating flesh. Bernini was twenty-three; the group stunned Rome into realizing that Baroque sculpture had outrun the limits of Hellenistic virtuosity.
1625
Europe
Apollo and Daphne
by Gian Lorenzo Bernini
Gian Lorenzo Bernini · 1625 · Galleria Borghese
Bernini caught the instant Daphne's fingers sprout leaves and her feet root into bark, her scream frozen just as Apollo's hand grazes her ribcage. He was twenty-six when he finished it and already the most technically audacious sculptor in Rome.
1625
Islamic World
Tomb of I'timād-ud-Daulah
Mughal · 1622–1628 · Agra
"The Baby Taj" — the first Mughal tomb clad entirely in white marble and inlaid with pietra dura. Built by Nur Jahan for her father; the stylistic prototype for the Taj Mahal eight years later.
1632
Europe
The Anatomy Lesson of Dr. Nicolaes Tulp
by Rembrandt
Dutch Golden Age · 1632
A public dissection of an executed criminal, witnessed by seven guild surgeons. Rembrandt was 26; it made his reputation.
1633
Europe
The Garden of Love
by Peter Paul Rubens
Peter Paul Rubens · 1633 · Museo del Prado
A riotous outdoor party Rubens painted after his second marriage — his young wife Hélène Fourment appears three times among the guests. Cupids tug couples together while a fountain with the Three Graces soaks the scene in divinely sanctioned pleasure.
1637
Europe
The Cheat with the Ace of Diamonds
by Georges de La Tour
c. 1636–1638 · Oil on canvas · Sully Wing
Four figures, four glances, one con. A naïve young nobleman is about to lose his fortune to the cheat on the left, who has pulled an ace from his belt. The courtesan and her servant are in on it; only the young man isn't looking at anyone.
1637
Islamic World
Tomb of Jahangir
Mughal · 1637 · Lahore
Jahangir — Akbar's son, Shah Jahan's father — lies under white marble inlaid with pietra dura flowers, in a garden tomb of red sandstone. The minarets at the corners rise but never touch the roof.
1637
South Asia
Punakha Dzong
Bhutan · 1637 · Punakha
Bhutan's second dzong, built at the confluence of two rivers and still the winter home of the monastic body. Its five-story central utse leans out over the water; inside, eighteenth-century murals of the Buddha's life glow in whale-oil lamplight.
1638
Europe
Self-Portrait as the Allegory of Painting
by Artemisia Gentileschi
Baroque · c. 1638–1639
Artemisia paints herself in the act of painting — arms muscled, sleeves pushed up. She was the first woman admitted to the Accademia di Belle Arti di Firenze.
1640
Africa
Fasil Ghebbi
Ethiopian · 17th c. · Gondar
A fortress-city of stone castles built by a succession of Solomonic emperors. The European-looking keeps are misleading — they're Ethiopian invention, not import.
1641
Islamic World
Shalamar Gardens
Mughal · Shah Jahan · 1641 · Lahore
Three terraces of Persian-style paradise garden, 400 fountains fed by a canal that once ran from the Himalayas. Listed in danger after colonial-era road-widening destroyed the outer walls.
1641
Islamic World
Wazir Khan Mosque
Mughal · 1641 · Lahore
A small neighborhood mosque entirely encrusted in Persian kashikari tilework whose floral panels are unmatched in subcontinental Islamic art. Commissioned by Shah Jahan's governor of Punjab, it is the premier showcase of seventeenth-century Mughal ceramic craft.
1642
Europe
The Night Watch
by Rembrandt
Dutch Golden Age · Rembrandt · 1642
A militia portrait transformed into a theatrical scene — figures stepping forward, a drumbeat, a girl glowing in the crowd. Not actually set at night.
1642
South Asia
Taj Mahal
Mughal · 1632–1653 · Agra
A tomb Shah Jahan built for his wife Mumtaz Mahal, who died in childbirth. The marble is inlaid with 28 kinds of gem and stone.
1645
East Asia
Potala Palace
Tibetan · 1645 onward · Lhasa
Thirteen stories of winter residence for the Dalai Lamas — a thousand rooms, ten thousand shrines, two hundred thousand statues. Built where a 7th-century fort once stood.
1647
Islamic World
Chehel Sotoun
Safavid · 1647 · Isfahan
A pavilion of "forty columns" — twenty real wooden ones plus their reflections in the long rectangular pool. Interior halls are painted with the Shah's battles and banquets.
1647
Islamic World
Shah Jahan Mosque, Thatta
Mughal · 1647 · Sindh, Pakistan
Shah Jahan built this mosque in thanks for the town's shelter after a revolt. Ninety-three domes and thirty-three arches are said to amplify the imam's voice so the entire congregation can hear without microphones — a feat of Mughal acoustical engineering.
1648
South Asia
Red Fort
Mughal · Shah Jahan · 1648 · Delhi
Red sandstone walls two kilometers around. Inside, the Diwan-i-Khas held the Peacock Throne until Nadir Shah of Persia took it to Isfahan in 1739.
1650
Africa
Kente Cloth
Asante & Ewe · 17th c. onward · Ghana
Hand-woven silk and cotton strips sewn together into royal cloth. Each pattern has a name and a proverb behind it.
1650
Africa
Akan Goldweights
Asante / Akan · 15th–19th c. · Ghana
Brass figurines used to weigh gold dust. Each one is also a proverb — the weight you balance against is a riddle.
1650
Africa
Ndop — Kuba King Portrait
Kuba Kingdom · 17th c. onward · DR Congo
Wooden royal portraits showing each Kuba king with a personal emblem — a drum, an anvil, a game board. Stand-ins who keep the king present after death.
1650
Africa
Baule Sculpture
Baule · 17th c. onward · Côte d'Ivoire
Delicate wooden figures of "spirit spouses" from a parallel world, and calm portrait masks carved for Goli festivals. Among the most influential African sculptural traditions.
1650
Europe
Self-Portraits by Rembrandt
by Rembrandt
17th century · Etching
Rembrandt made over forty etched self-portraits across his lifetime. The Louvre holds a remarkable set. In ink on paper he is looser and more playful than in his painted selves — pulling faces, squinting, trying on hats.
1650
Islamic World
Topkapı Palace
Ottoman · 15th–19th c. · Istanbul
The sultans' residence for 400 years. Unlike European palaces, it's a scatter of pavilions in a walled garden — closer to a fortified village.
1650
South Asia
Amber Fort
Rajput · 16th–18th c. · Jaipur
Hilltop fort-palace of the Kachhwaha dynasty. The Hall of Mirrors inside glitters with thousands of convex glass inlays; a single candle lights the whole room.
1650
South Asia
Mughal Miniature Painting
Mughal · 16th–18th c. · North India
Album leaves commissioned by the Mughal emperors — historical chronicles, portraits, and natural history drawn with jeweller's precision.
1650
Americas
Navajo Weaving
Diné · 17th c. onward · American Southwest
Tightly woven wool rugs and wearing blankets — the weaver puts a deliberate flaw into each one, a ch'į́įdii line, so the spirit of the piece has a way out.
1651
Europe
Fountain of the Four Rivers
by Gian Lorenzo Bernini
Gian Lorenzo Bernini · 1651 · Piazza Navona, Rome
Allegories of the Nile, Ganges, Danube, and Río de la Plata lounge around a hollowed travertine crag supporting an Egyptian obelisk. The Nile covers his face because, according to legend, the source of the river was still unknown — or because he can't bear to look at Borromini's church opposite.
1652
Europe
The Ecstasy of Saint Teresa
by Gian Lorenzo Bernini
Gian Lorenzo Bernini · 1652 · Cornaro Chapel, Rome
The Carmelite mystic swoons in midair as a smiling angel lifts a gold-tipped arrow above her heart. Bernini turned the entire chapel into a theater: the Cornaro patrons watch from opera-box side panels, a hidden window rains gilded light down on the marble.
1653
Africa
Cape Coast Castle
Colonial fortification · 1653 onward · Ghana
A whitewashed castle on the Gulf of Guinea used for the Atlantic slave trade — enslaved Africans passed through its "Door of No Return." Now a museum, with whitewash still yearly.
1656
Europe
Las Meninas
by Diego Velázquez
Spanish Golden Age · Velázquez · 1656
The Infanta and her attendants — but who is the painting for? The king and queen appear only in the mirror. Foucault wrote an essay on that mirror.
1656
South Asia
Gol Gumbaz
Adil Shahi · 1656 · Bijapur
Tomb of Mohammed Adil Shah. The dome is the second-largest in the world, and the whispering gallery underneath it repeats a voice seven times.
1656
South Asia
Jama Masjid, Delhi
Mughal · Shah Jahan · 1656 · Delhi
The largest mosque in India — a red sandstone and marble courtyard for 25,000. Built in the same decade as the Taj Mahal, by the same emperor.
1659
East Asia
Shugakuin Imperial Villa
Edo period · 1659 · Kyoto
Emperor Go-Mizunoo retired here to design tiered gardens climbing the foothills of Mount Hiei. The upper villa's dammed pond reflects borrowed scenery of the surrounding mountains — the purest surviving example of Japanese shakkei landscape design.
1660
Europe
The Milkmaid
by Johannes Vermeer
Dutch Golden Age · c. 1660
A kitchen maid pours milk into a bowl. Morning light from the window. The whole painting is about the thin stream of milk and the pause it creates.
1660
Europe
View of Delft
by Johannes Vermeer
Dutch Golden Age · c. 1660–1661
Vermeer's hometown across the water, clouds moving overhead. Proust called it "the most beautiful painting in the world" and had one of his characters die looking at it.
1665
Europe
Girl with a Pearl Earring
by Johannes Vermeer
Dutch Golden Age · Vermeer · c. 1665
Not a portrait but a "tronie" — a study of an imagined figure. The pearl itself is two quick strokes of white paint. We don't know who she was.
1670
Europe
The Lacemaker
by Johannes Vermeer
Dutch Golden Age · c. 1669–1671 · Louvre, Paris
Only ten inches tall. A girl bent over her bobbins in concentrated silence — Vermeer's smallest canvas and one of his most intense studies of attention.
1670
South Asia
Patan Durbar Square
Malla dynasty · 17th century · Lalitpur, Nepal
The royal plaza of the Malla kings of Patan, edged with Newar pagodas and a stone Krishna Mandir carved in imitation of north Indian shikharas. Several temples collapsed in 2015 and have been reconstructed with timber dowels rather than steel.
1670
East Asia
Kakiemon Porcelain
Edo Japan · 17th c. onward · Arita
Overglaze enamels on a milk-white ground. The Kakiemon workshop at Arita invented a palette — iron red, green, yellow, blue — that Meissen and Chantilly spent decades trying to copy.
1673
Islamic World
Badshahi Mosque
Mughal · 1673 · Lahore
Aurangzeb's great imperial mosque, faced with red sandstone and inlaid with white marble. Its courtyard holds 100,000 worshippers; for 313 years it was the largest mosque in the world.
1680
Europe
Palace of Versailles
French Baroque · 1661 onward · France
Louis XIV's hunting lodge turned seat of French monarchy. The Hall of Mirrors reflects the west-facing garden at sunset; every piece of furniture was once pure silver.
1690
East Asia
Ink Landscape (Self-Portrait)
by Shitao
Qing · Shitao · late 17th c.
Shitao paints himself sitting on a rock, brush poised, surrounded by hills he is still deciding to render. A Ming prince turned Buddhist monk; his theoretical writings on painting are still studied.
1692
South Asia
Paro Taktsang (Tiger's Nest Monastery)
Bhutan · 1692 · Paro
Perched on a cliff 900 meters above the Paro valley, on the ledge where Guru Rinpoche was said to have meditated for three years, three months, three weeks, three days, and three hours. A 1998 fire destroyed most of it; the rebuild faithfully repeats the original timber joinery.
1698
Europe
The Régent Diamond
140.64 carats · Galerie d'Apollon
Found in India around 1698, smuggled by a slave who hid it in a wound in his leg, sold to Thomas Pitt (grandfather of William Pitt the Elder), and eventually bought by the French crown. Worn by Louis XV, Louis XVI, and set into Napoleon's sword. Still considered the most perfectly cut historical diamond in the world.
1700
Africa
Koutammakou (Takienta Tower-Houses)
Batammariba · 17th century onward · Togo/Benin
Fortified two-story mud towers with conical thatched granary turrets, built by the Batammariba on the Togo-Benin border. Each house is read as a human body — entrance mouth, granary head — and its construction ritualized every stage of a family's life.
1700
South Asia
Golden Temple
Sikh · 16th–19th c. · Amritsar
The holiest site of Sikhism. Marble walls inlaid with gemstone, upper floors covered in gold leaf, the whole thing floating on a sacred pool.
1700
South Asia
Thanjavur Painting
Nayaka / Maratha · 16th–19th c. · Tamil Nadu
Devotional paintings built up in gesso, then layered in gold leaf, semi-precious stones, and mirrored glass. Surface so encrusted the figures seem to float off the board.
1700
East Asia
Moon Jar
Joseon white porcelain · 17th–18th century · Korea
Large round jars fired in two halves and joined at the equator, allowing subtle asymmetries to soften each moon-shaped silhouette. Modern Korean painters including Kim Whanki built entire careers around the jar's quiet poise.
1700
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Tā Moko
Māori · pre-European onward · New Zealand
Tattoos chiseled (not punctured) into the skin, carrying genealogy and rank. Each design is unique to the person who wears it.
1700
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Hei-tiki
Māori · pre-European onward · New Zealand
Nephrite pendants of a human figure worn around the neck, passed down through generations. Each is named; each gains mana from the people who wear it.
1700
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Rongorongo
Rapa Nui · pre-contact? · Easter Island
Glyphs carved onto wooden tablets — one of the few independent writing systems in human history. Still undeciphered; most tablets were burned by missionaries.
1700
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Kris
Malay Archipelago · 13th c. onward
An asymmetric wavy dagger with nickel-iron pattern welding. Considered an heirloom with a spirit of its own — some can be named, inherited, or banned from leaving an island.
1703
Islamic World
Mausoleum of Moulay Ismail
Alaouite · late 17th century · Meknes
Burial place of the sultan who built seven hundred palaces and commanded an army of black guards numbering 150,000. The sanctuary is one of the few in Morocco non-Muslims may enter; its painted cedar ceiling and tilework glitter around four clocks presented by Louis XIV.
1710
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Paoay Church
Spanish colonial · 1710 · Ilocos Norte, Philippines
An 'earthquake Baroque' church with enormous coralline buttresses that curve outward like flying sleepers, bracing it against the region's seismic activity. Its bell tower stands detached in case of collapse.
1717
Europe
Pilgrimage to Cythera
by Antoine Watteau
Antoine Watteau · 1717 · Musée du Louvre
Watteau's reception piece for the Académie: couples linger and depart from the island of love, a gilded barge waiting, cupids swirling overhead. The genre he invented — the fête galante — gave eighteenth-century painting its register of wistful pleasure.
1722
Europe
Crown of Louis XV
1722 · Silver gilt, originally set with the crown jewels · Galerie d'Apollon
Made for the coronation of the twelve-year-old Louis XV at Reims. The original diamonds — including the Régent, now displayed nearby — were removed after the ceremony and replaced with paste copies, which are what you see today.
1734
South Asia
Jantar Mantar
Rajput · Jai Singh II · 1734 · Jaipur
An observatory of huge masonry instruments — sundials ninety feet tall, instruments to locate any star. Built to correct the drifting calendars of Mughal India.
1742
Europe
The Marly Horses
by Guillaume Coustou
1739–1745 · Marble · Cour Marly
Commissioned for the royal estate of Marly, where Louis XV wanted rearing horses at the entrance to his watering pond. Each is over 3.5 meters tall. They stood outdoors for nearly two centuries before being brought inside in 1984.
1750
Africa
Golden Stool of the Asante
Asante · 18th c. · Ghana
Sacred seat that is said to contain the soul of the Asante nation. No one — not even the king — sits on it. A British demand to do so in 1900 sparked war.
1750
Africa
Adinkra Symbols
Akan / Asante · 18th c. onward · Ghana
A system of visual proverbs stamped or woven into cloth — each glyph carrying a name and an aphorism. Worn at funerals; later embraced globally as a Pan-African vocabulary.
1750
East Asia
Summer Palace
Qing dynasty · 1750 onward · Beijing
Three square kilometres of gardens and pavilions around Kunming Lake. Sacked by Anglo-French forces in 1860, looted again by Boxer-era troops in 1900.
1750
East Asia
Netsuke
Edo Japan · 17th–19th c.
Miniature ivory or wood toggles that held purses to kimono sashes. Frogs, rats, mythic beasts — detailed enough to demand a magnifying glass.
1750
East Asia
Red and White Plum Blossoms
by Ogata Kōrin
Edo Japan · Ogata Kōrin · early 18th c.
A pair of folding screens: two plum trees, red and white, lean toward a stylized silver river of whorls. Rinpa-school decorative painting at its most distilled.
1750
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Māori Wharenui
Aotearoa · 18th c. onward · New Zealand
A carved meeting house represents an ancestor's body — ridgepole as spine, rafters as ribs. Every panel tells a story in whakairo carving.
1750
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Vigan
Spanish colonial · 16th–19th century · Ilocos Sur, Philippines
A coastal trading town laid out on a grid before the 1572 Spanish conquest, blending Chinese, Ilocano, and Iberian elements into a distinct Philippine colonial vernacular. Calle Crisologo's cobbled streets and bahay-na-bato mansions survived Allied bombing because a Japanese general refused to burn it.
1760
East Asia
Dream of the Red Chamber
by Cao Xueqin
Qing China · Cao Xueqin · 18th c.
A classic Chinese novel in 120 chapters, commonly illustrated from Qing to today — the Jia family's decline rendered in garden pavilions, dying flowers, a jade inscribed with fate.
1765
East Asia
Nishiki-e (Brocade Prints)
Edo Japan · 1765 onward
Multi-color woodblock prints developed by Suzuki Harunobu in 1765 — the first polychrome ukiyo-e. Enabled Utamaro, Hokusai, Hiroshige to work in full color.
1767
Europe
The Swing
by Jean-Honoré Fragonard
Jean-Honoré Fragonard · 1767 · Wallace Collection
A young woman kicks off her slipper while her hidden lover peeks up her billowing dress — the jilted bishop pushing the swing behind her. The Baron de Saint-Julien commissioned it explicitly to stage this visual joke at his mistress's expense.
1782
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Wat Phra Kaew
Rattanakosin · 1782 onward · Bangkok
The royal temple inside the Grand Palace, home to the Emerald Buddha — carved from a single block of jade, robed differently for each season.
1784
Europe
Oath of the Horatii
by Jacques-Louis David
Jacques-Louis David · 1784 · Musée du Louvre
Three brothers swear on their father's swords to die for Rome while their women collapse in a curve of grief. Commissioned by Louis XVI and unveiled five years before the Revolution, the painting defined Neoclassical moral rigor for a generation.
1787
Europe
The Death of Socrates
by Jacques-Louis David
Jacques-Louis David · 1787 · Metropolitan Museum of Art
Socrates reaches for the hemlock while lecturing his grieving disciples on immortality, one finger stabbing heavenward. David painted it on the eve of the Revolution; Reynolds called it the greatest modern history painting.
1788
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Wat Pho
Rattanakosin · 1788 · Bangkok
Home of the 46-meter gold-leafed Reclining Buddha and Thailand's first public university, where medical texts were carved into marble plaques around the courtyard. Traditional Thai massage is still taught in an attached school founded in 1955.
1790
East Asia
Prints of Kitagawa Utamaro
by Kitagawa Utamaro
Edo Japan · 1790s
Utamaro specialized in the bijin-ga — studies of beautiful women — often just head and shoulders, in close-up. Scandalously intimate for the time.
1793
Europe
Psyche Revived by Cupid's Kiss
by Antonio Canova
Neoclassical · 1793 · Louvre, Paris
Canova's marble of the moment Cupid wakes Psyche with a kiss. Designed to be circled — every angle offers a different composition.
1793
Europe
The Death of Marat
by Jacques-Louis David
French Neoclassicism · 1793
Marat in his bathtub, stabbed by Charlotte Corday, pen still in hand. David painted him as a secular martyr — a revolutionary Pietà for a Republic that had killed its king.
1794
East Asia
Actor Prints of Sharaku
by Tōshūsai Sharaku
Edo Japan · Tōshūsai Sharaku · 1794–1795
Kabuki actors caught mid-gesture, their exaggerated faces rendered in crisp black outline against mica backgrounds. Sharaku worked for ten months and then vanished.
1796
East Asia
Hwaseong Fortress
Joseon · 1796 · Suwon, Korea
