Jeongjo built this 5.7-kilometer wall to honor his executed father; the young scholar Jeong Yak-yong designed an innovative pulley-based crane to place each granite block. A complete construction manual recording every worker's wages survives from 1801.
1799
South Asia
Hawa Mahal
Rajput · 1799 · Jaipur
The "Palace of Winds" — a honeycomb facade of 953 latticed windows, built so the women of the court could watch the street without being seen.

1800 onward95 works

1800
Africa
Kuba Appliqué Textiles
Kuba Kingdom · 17th c. onward · DR Congo
Raffia cloth embroidered with cut-pile geometrics. Men weave the base; women embroider. No two patterns repeat — each is a one-time composition.
1800
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Batik
Javanese · traditional · Indonesia
Patterns drawn in hot wax on cotton, then dyed — the wax resisting the color. Javanese court batiks reserved some motifs for royalty on pain of death.
1800
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Kūkaʻilimoku
Hawaiian · 18th–early 19th century · Hawaiʻi
Feathered figures of the war god of Kamehameha I, their faces sheathed in tens of thousands of red and yellow bird feathers meticulously tied to a wicker armature. Grimacing pearl-shell eyes and dog-tooth mouths dramatize the god's ferocity.
1801
Europe
Napoleon Crossing the Alps
by Jacques-Louis David
Jacques-Louis David · 1801 · Château de Malmaison
David invented the cinematic pose — rearing horse, windswept cape, outstretched finger — that the actual crossing lacked. Napoleon, who had used a mule, told David to paint him 'calm on a fiery horse' and handed him five replicas to produce.
1806
Europe
The Coronation of Napoleon
by Jacques-Louis David
French Neoclassicism · 1805–1807 · Louvre, Paris
Nearly ten meters wide. Napoleon, having just placed the crown on his own head, is about to crown Joséphine. David inserted Napoleon's mother in the grandstand though she refused to attend.
1809
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Wat Arun
Rattanakosin · 1809 · Bangkok
The 'Temple of Dawn' across the Chao Phraya from Bangkok, its central prang encrusted with broken Chinese porcelain imported as ship ballast. Its five towers form a stone mandala of Mount Meru that Rama II rebuilt from a smaller Ayutthaya-era shrine.
1814
Europe
The Third of May 1808
by Francisco Goya
Spanish Romanticism · 1814
A Madrid citizen, arms flung wide, faces a French firing squad in lantern-light. Painted as an accusation — art reporting on atrocity for the first time.
1814
Europe
La Grande Odalisque
by Jean-Auguste-Dominique Ingres
French Neoclassicism · 1814 · Louvre, Paris
A concubine reclines on impossibly long, boneless anatomy. Critics counted extra vertebrae; Ingres was not interested in bones — he was interested in line.
1818
Europe
Wanderer above the Sea of Fog
by Caspar David Friedrich
German Romanticism · 1818
A man stands on a rocky peak, his back to us, looking into swirling cloud. Romanticism in one image — the sublime, and the lone self against it.
1818
Europe
The Raft of the Medusa
by Théodore Géricault
French Romanticism · 1818–1819 · Louvre, Paris
A real recent disaster: 147 men cut loose on a raft off Senegal after the captain took the lifeboats. Fifteen survived. Géricault visited morgues and built a model in his studio.
1821
Europe
The Hay Wain
by John Constable
English Romanticism · 1821
A farm cart in a shallow stream on a summer afternoon. Nothing is happening. Constable turned rural quiet into monumental painting.
1821
Europe
Saturn Devouring His Son
by Francisco Goya
Black Paintings · 1819–1823
Goya painted it on the wall of his dining room. Old, deaf, sick, he let Saturn eat his son with hollow eyes. Transferred to canvas after his death; not meant for anyone to see.
1830
Europe
Liberty Leading the People
by Eugène Delacroix
French Romanticism · 1830 · Louvre, Paris
Painted after the July Revolution that overthrew Charles X. Liberty strides over the barricades, bare-breasted, bayonet and tricolor raised. The boy with pistols later became Hugo's Gavroche.
1831
East Asia
The Great Wave off Kanagawa
by Katsushika Hokusai
Edo Japan · Hokusai · c. 1831
A woodblock print sold for the price of a bowl of noodles. Mount Fuji sits small and steady under the claw of the wave. Hokusai was in his seventies.
1831
East Asia
Fine Wind, Clear Morning (Red Fuji)
by Katsushika Hokusai
Edo Japan · Hokusai · c. 1830–1832
Hokusai's other famous Fuji — a single triangular form lit crimson by morning sun, clouds streaming off the summit. From the same series as the Great Wave.
1835
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Waka (Māori War Canoe)
Māori · 18th–19th c. · Aotearoa New Zealand
Large war canoes carved from a single tōtara trunk. Prows and sternposts carved with ancestors; the hull painted ochre. Rowed by eighty paddlers, two drummers, a chief at the stern.
1839
Europe
The Fighting Temeraire
by J. M. W. Turner
English Romanticism · 1839
A storied warship, veteran of Trafalgar, tugged by a smudgy little steam tug to the breaker's yard. Turner's elegy for the age of sail — voted Britain's favorite painting.
1850
Africa
Senufo Sculpture
Senufo · West Africa
Elongated female figures and carved divination stools used by the Poro and Sandogo societies in Côte d'Ivoire and Mali.
1850
Africa
Ibeji Twin Figures
Yoruba · 19th c. onward · Nigeria
When a twin dies, a carved figure stands in — fed, dressed, and cared for like the living. The Yoruba have one of the highest twinning rates in the world.
1850
Africa
Asante Jewelry
Akan / Asante · 19th c. · Ghana
Gold jewelry for the chief and his court — beads, pectorals, rings cast with the same lost-wax technique as the goldweights. Sumptuary law regulated who could wear what.
1850
Africa
Tuareg Metalwork
Tuareg · 18th c. onward · Sahara
Silver crosses, amulet cases, saddle fittings. Each Tuareg town has a signature cross shape; the Agadez cross is the most widely reproduced.
1850
Africa
Ikenga
Igbo · pre-1900 · southeast Nigeria
Personal shrines carved as horned male figures, each one owned by a man and activated through sacrifice to embody the 'right hand' — the strength that accomplishes things. When the owner dies the Ikenga is split in two.
1850
South Asia
Mahabodhi Temple
Gupta → restored 19th c. · Bodh Gaya
Built at the spot the Buddha attained enlightenment under a Bodhi tree. A direct descendant of that tree still grows beside the temple.
1850
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Wayang Golek
Sundanese · 17th c. onward · West Java
Carved wooden rod puppets, fully dressed in miniature batik. Performed in all-night epics of the Ramayana and Mahabharata — the dalang puppeteer plays every voice.
1850
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Tau Tau
Toraja · Sulawesi, Indonesia
Carved wooden effigies of the dead, placed in cliff-face galleries above Toraja villages. Each tau tau faces outward and is redressed periodically by descendants; some are centuries old.
1850
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Okir Carving
Maranao · 18th century onward · Mindanao, Philippines
Curvilinear vegetal ornament and geometric naga motifs carved into wooden house beams, brass betel boxes, and the okir frontispiece of the Torogan. The tradition survives Spanish and American colonial pressure as a distinct non-Christian Filipino art.
1850
Americas
Northwest Coast Totem Poles
Haida, Tlingit, Kwakwaka'wakw · 18th–20th c.
Cedar poles carved with family lineage, clan animals, and narrated stories. Each is a library — readable top to bottom by those who know the figures.
1850
Americas
Haida Argillite Carving
Haida · 19th c. onward · Haida Gwaii
Black shale carvings only the Haida may quarry, developed as a trade art for European sailors. Ship figureheads, pipes, and eventually complex narrative panels.
1857
Europe
The Gleaners
by Jean-François Millet
Realism · 1857
Three peasant women stoop to pick up leftover grain after the harvest. The wealthy hated it — it looked like socialism. Millet painted rural labor as dignified, not picturesque.
1861
Europe
The Napoleon III Apartments
1861 · Richelieu Wing
Not a single object but an entire suite of state rooms — crimson silk, gilded stucco, chandeliers heavy enough to frighten the ceiling. These were the ceremonial apartments of the Minister of State under the Second Empire. Walk in and the museum disappears.
1863
Europe
Le Déjeuner sur l'herbe
by Édouard Manet
Early Modern · 1863
A nude woman picnics with two fully dressed men. The scandal wasn't the nudity — it was that she looks directly at the viewer, unashamed.
1863
Europe
Olympia
by Édouard Manet
Early Modern · 1863
A courtesan reclining nude, a Black maid bringing flowers, a cat raising its tail. The Salon hung it high to keep people from slashing it.
1870
Africa
Sowei Helmet Mask
Mende · 19th c. onward · Sierra Leone
A black helmet mask worn in the Sande women's society initiation — the only African mask tradition danced exclusively by women, representing ideals of beauty and comportment.
1870
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Torogan House
Maranao · 19th century · Lanao, Philippines
An enormous elevated wooden house for the sultan's family, its floor held aloft by two-meter carved tree trunks whose projecting panolong prows evoke ships. Inside, one single gigantic room divides only by textile hangings and movable panels.
1870
Americas
Crooked Beak of Heaven Mask
Kwakwaka'wakw · 19th c. · Pacific Northwest
A transformation mask from the Hamatsa winter dance. The outer "cannibal bird" beak opens with strings during the dance to reveal a human face within — the return of the initiate.
1872
Europe
Impression, Sunrise
Impressionism · Monet · 1872 · Le Havre
The painting that gave a movement its name — a hostile critic meant "impression" as an insult. Hazy harbor, red sun, no detail that isn't a brushstroke.
1876
Europe
Dance at Le Moulin de la Galette
by Pierre-Auguste Renoir
Pierre-Auguste Renoir · 1876 · Musée d'Orsay
A Sunday afternoon at the Montmartre dance hall, sunlight filtering through acacia leaves onto the dancers in dapples Renoir invented a new vocabulary of color to paint. His friends posed for it over several months.
1880
Africa
Zulu Beadwork
Zulu · 19th c. onward · South Africa
Glass trade beads sewn onto leather and cotton in geometric patterns — colors and combinations encoding courtship messages ("love letters" in bead).
1880
Africa
Chiwara Headdress
Bamana · 19th–20th century · Mali
A roan antelope carved in openwork wood worn by young men who danced in pairs at planting season. The Bamana say Chiwara is the mythical being who first taught humans to farm, bent over the earth like the antelope's graceful curve.
1880
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Malangan Carvings
New Ireland · 19th c. onward · Papua New Guinea
Elaborately painted wooden figures carved for a single memorial ceremony, then deliberately destroyed or allowed to rot. Their knowledge was owned as intellectual property; only certain carvers could make certain forms.
1880
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Bulul
Ifugao · 19th century · northern Luzon, Philippines
Carved wooden rice-granary guardians placed in pairs on sacks of grain and anointed annually with pig's blood and rice wine. Ifugao priests chant lineages of ancestors while invoking each Bulul; the more darkened with offerings, the more powerful.
1881
Europe
Luncheon of the Boating Party
by Pierre-Auguste Renoir
Pierre-Auguste Renoir · 1881 · The Phillips Collection
Renoir's friends — among them his future wife Aline, caressing a little dog — laze on a restaurant terrace overlooking the Seine. It is impressionism at its most convivial: no protest, no weather, just a warm afternoon extended indefinitely.
1882
Africa
Kasubi Tombs
Buganda kingdom · 1882 · Uganda
The burial palace of four kabakas of Buganda, a vast thatched dome called Muzibu-Azaala-Mpanga supported by rings of wooden columns wrapped in bark cloth. Fire destroyed the main structure in 2010 and a painstaking reconstruction is ongoing.
1885
Europe
A Sunday on La Grande Jatte
by Georges Seurat
Post-Impressionism · Seurat · 1884–1886
Pointillism's founding canvas — ten feet wide, built entirely from tiny dots. Parisians on a Sunday afternoon, rendered like nothing before.
1885
Europe
The Potato Eaters
by Vincent van Gogh
Early Van Gogh · 1885 · Nuenen
Dark, muddy, deliberately ugly. Van Gogh wrote that he wanted to show hands that had dug the earth eating the potatoes those hands had grown. His breakthrough, though critics hated it.
1888
Europe
Sunflowers
by Vincent van Gogh
Post-Impressionism · 1888
Painted in Arles to decorate Gauguin's guest bedroom. Van Gogh made seven versions; in letters, he called them an ode to gratitude.
1888
Europe
Café Terrace at Night
by Vincent van Gogh
Post-Impressionism · 1888 · Arles
The yellow terrace still stands in Arles (renamed after the painting). No black in the night sky — deep cobalt blue everywhere instead. Van Gogh's first major work done at night.
1888
Islamic World
Nasir al-Mulk Mosque
Qajar · 1888 · Shiraz
The "Pink Mosque" — stained-glass windows scatter colored light across Persian carpets every morning. Built late in the Islamic architectural tradition, as a kind of summary.
1889
Europe
The Starry Night
by Vincent van Gogh
Post-Impressionism · Van Gogh · 1889
Painted from the window of his asylum room at Saint-Rémy, from memory. The village below is imagined; the swirling sky he said he could actually see.
1890
Islamic World
Bahia Palace
Late Alaouite · 1866–1900 · Marrakech
A palace for the grand vizier Ahmed ben Moussa and his four wives, its 150 rooms arranged around courtyards of green tilework, painted cedar ceilings, and carved plaster. The name means 'the brilliant' — a name intended to impress visitors upon arrival.
1890
Americas
Ghost Dance Shirt
Lakota · 1890 · Great Plains
Hide shirts painted with stars and eagles, worn in the Ghost Dance — believed to turn aside bullets. Shirts were collected from the dead at Wounded Knee.
1892
Europe
The Thinker
by Auguste Rodin
Modern · Rodin · 1880–1904
Originally a small figure above The Gates of Hell — Dante, looking down at his Inferno. Enlarged and cast on its own, it became a universal emblem of thought.
1893
Europe
The Scream
by Edvard Munch
Expressionism · Edvard Munch · 1893
Munch wrote in his diary: "I sensed an infinite scream passing through nature." The sky's red may be from the Krakatoa eruption ten years earlier.
1897
Europe
The Sleeping Gypsy
by Henri Rousseau
Henri Rousseau · 1897 · Museum of Modern Art
A dark-robed traveler sleeps beside her mandolin while a lion sniffs her face under a flat full moon. Rousseau, a self-taught customs clerk, offered to sell it to his hometown mayor for 200 francs; he was politely declined.
1899
Americas
Hopi Katsina Doll
Hopi · 19th c. onward · American Southwest
Carved cottonwood figures given to Hopi children to teach them the 400+ spirit beings of the Katsinam. Collectors later prized them so much the carving style changed to please buyers.
1900
Africa
Nkisi Power Figure
Kongo · 19th–20th c. · Central Africa
Wooden figures bristling with nails and blades. Each nail driven in activated a pledge, a curse, or a contract witnessed by the spirit inside.
1900
Africa
Fang Reliquary (Byeri)
Fang · 19th–20th c. · Gabon / Cameroon
Carved guardian heads placed atop boxes of ancestral bones. Picasso saw one in 1907 and the Demoiselles d'Avignon followed.
1900
Africa
Akuaba
Akan · traditional · Ghana
Wooden fertility figures carried by women hoping to conceive. Small, abstracted, round-faced. Carved also now for collectors; the sacred ones are never sold.
1900
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Asmat Carving
Asmat people · Papua, Indonesia
Bisj poles and shields carved from mangrove with sharpened bone. Each pole commemorates a death and demands it be avenged.
1900
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Mentawai Body Tattoo
Mentawai people · millennia · Indonesia
A Mentawai sikerei (shaman) in full body tattoo. Designs carry rank and history; the practice is said to be one of the oldest continuous tattoo traditions on earth.
1900
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Thai Silk
Thai · 13th c. onward
Hand-reeled mulberry silk dyed and woven into patterned sashes, sarongs, and court textiles. Nearly extinct by 1945; an American expatriate named Jim Thompson revived the industry before vanishing in Malaysia.
1900
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Hmong Textile
Hmong · traditional · SE Asia
Reverse-appliqué and cross-stitch panels in jewel colors — "flower cloth" (paj ntaub). Often incorporates figures and stories; since the 1970s used to document Hmong migration and war.
1900
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Tapa Cloth
Polynesian bark cloth · pre-European to present · Fiji/Tonga/Samoa
Non-woven fabric beaten from paper-mulberry bark and stamped, painted, or smoked with fine geometric patterns. Each region of Polynesia developed its own grammar; Fijian gatu vakaviti like this one are made for weddings and funeral exchanges.
1900
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Malanggan Carvings
New Ireland · 19th–20th century · Papua New Guinea
Painted wooden figures made for a single memorial ceremony and then deliberately allowed to rot or be sold off. European museums hold hundreds of examples; the meaning of each belonged solely to the man who commissioned it and died with the performance.
1900
Americas
Zuni Fetishes
A:shiwi (Zuni) · traditional · New Mexico
Small stone carvings of animals — bear, eagle, mountain lion — each holding a spirit and often wrapped with an offering bundle of turquoise or shell.
1907
Europe
The Kiss
Vienna Secession · Klimt · 1907–1908
Gold leaf and oil on canvas. A couple fused inside a shimmering shroud of pattern — Klimt's answer to the new century.
1907
Europe
Les Demoiselles d'Avignon
by Pablo Picasso
Proto-Cubism · 1907
Five Barcelona prostitutes, faces half-African mask, bodies fractured into angular planes. Picasso hid it for years; when it emerged it had already ended the 19th century.
1910
Europe
The Dance
by Henri Matisse
Fauvism · 1909–1910
Five red figures hold hands in a frenzied ring against blue sky and green earth. Matisse called the composition "the highest point of the story" — the first great Modernist painting of pure color.
1910
Europe
The Dream (Rousseau)
by Henri Rousseau
Henri Rousseau · 1910 · Museum of Modern Art
Rousseau's final completed work: a nude Yadwigha reclines on a Second Empire sofa that floats through a moonlit jungle of invented foliage, a snake charmer piping behind. He insisted the sofa's incongruity was perfectly explained by the word 'dream.'
1911
Europe
Water Lilies
by Claude Monet
Impressionism · Monet · 1896–1926 · Giverny
Two hundred and fifty paintings of the pond in his garden, late in life, eyes failing. The largest canvases wrap around the room at the Orangerie.
1911
Europe
I and the Village
by Marc Chagall
Cubist / Expressionist · 1911
A green-faced peasant and a white goat exchange a look across a village that floats and spins. Chagall's memory of his hometown of Vitebsk, filtered through Paris.
1911
Europe
The Red Studio
by Henri Matisse
Henri Matisse · 1911 · Museum of Modern Art
Matisse soaked his Issy studio in uniform Venetian red, leaving his own paintings as the only color notes on the wall. He had begun with green and blue underneath; the radical decision to cover everything in a single pigment recast what a painting could be.
1912
South Asia
Mysore Palace
Indo-Saracenic · 1912 · Karnataka
The current incarnation — fourth to stand on the site — is a wedding cake of Hindu, Mughal, Rajput, and Gothic elements. Lit up nightly by nearly a hundred thousand bulbs.
1913
Europe
Composition VII
by Wassily Kandinsky
Abstract · 1913
Kandinsky called it the most complex work he ever painted — over thirty preparatory studies. Motifs of resurrection, flood, and last judgment dissolve into color and line.
1913
South Asia
Victoria Memorial
British Raj · 1906–1921 · Kolkata
A white marble domed memorial to Queen Victoria, built at the height of imperial self-regard. Now a museum of the Raj and the nationalism that ended it.
1921
Europe
The Elephant Celebes
by Max Ernst
Max Ernst · 1921 · Tate Modern
A giant metallic elephant based on a photograph of a Sudanese corn bin looms over a headless mannequin beside a mysterious fish-stalk. Ernst's breakthrough Surrealist work, painted before André Breton had even coined the term.
1922
Europe
Senecio
by Paul Klee
Paul Klee · 1922 · Kunstmuseum Basel
A geometrically segmented face painted in ochres and pinks, the eyes slightly mismatched so it never quite looks back. Klee claimed the title 'Old Man' (Senecio in Latin); the image belongs somewhere between clown, sun god, and child's drawing.
1929
Europe
The Treachery of Images
by René Magritte
Surrealism · 1929
A pipe painted with the words "Ceci n'est pas une pipe" (This is not a pipe). It isn't — it's a painting of one. Magritte's founding joke of Surrealism.
1930
Europe
Composition with Red, Blue and Yellow
by Piet Mondrian
De Stijl · 1930
Black lines, three primary colors, white negative space, canvas flat as the grid itself. Mondrian believed he had found the underlying logic of visible reality.
1930
Europe
American Gothic
by Grant Wood
Grant Wood · 1930 · Art Institute of Chicago
Wood's sister Nan and his dentist Byron McKeeby posed as a farmer and his daughter in front of a white-board Iowa house. Meant by Wood as a gentle tribute, the image has been parodied perhaps more than any other American painting.
1942
Europe
Nighthawks
by Edward Hopper
Edward Hopper · 1942 · Art Institute of Chicago
Four figures in a late-night Greenwich Avenue diner, insulated by its curved glass from the empty street outside. Painted weeks after Pearl Harbor, it fixed the look of twentieth-century American loneliness.
1943
Europe
Broadway Boogie Woogie
by Piet Mondrian
Piet Mondrian · 1943 · Museum of Modern Art
Mondrian's last completed painting, made after he fled wartime Europe for New York. The jittering yellow grid mimics Manhattan's street plan and the jazz he had fallen in love with; black bars are banished for the first time in decades.
1950
Africa
Ndebele House Painting
Ndebele · 20th century · South Africa
Exterior walls painted freehand by women in sharp-edged chevrons, diamonds, and vivid blocks of color, applied without grid or ruler. The practice emerged after the 1883 Ndebele defeat as a coded language of resistance preserved inside everyday domestic space.
1950
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Abelam Baba Mask
Abelam · 20th century · Sepik, Papua New Guinea
Spirit masks used in yam-harvest ceremonies celebrating the long ceremonial yams that Abelam men cultivate for years. The masks top painted ceremonial houses up to thirty meters tall, decorated with rows of ancestor faces in red, black, and white.
1950
Americas
Maria Martinez Blackware
by Maria Martinez
San Ildefonso Pueblo · 20th c. · New Mexico
Maria and her husband Julian revived and reinvented matte-on-black pueblo pottery. Their pots sit in every major American museum — and still on shelves at San Ildefonso.
1951
South Asia
Somnath Temple
Chaulukya → rebuilt 1951 · Gujarat
A Hindu pilgrimage site destroyed and rebuilt seven times — plundered most famously by Mahmud of Ghazni in 1025. The current temple was built after Indian independence, at the sea's edge.
1960
Africa
Osun-Osogbo Sacred Grove
Yoruba · 20th-c. revival of traditional · Nigeria
A forest along the Oshun River filled with sculptures revived by the Austrian-Yoruba artist Susanne Wenger and local carvers — the last surviving intact sacred grove of the Yoruba.
1960
Americas
Inuit Art
Inuit · 20th c. contemporary · Arctic
Carved soapstone, serpentine, and whalebone figures of hunters, spirits, and animals. A tradition fused out of older ivory carving and 20th-century print cooperatives like Cape Dorset.
1980
Africa
Shona Stone Sculpture
Shona · 20th c. onward · Zimbabwe
A post-colonial sculptural tradition carved from serpentine and springstone. Figures of spirits and ancestors, often semi-abstracted — now Zimbabwe's most recognized art export.
1986
Islamic World
Faisal Mosque
Modern Islamic · 1986 · Islamabad
A Turkish architect's vision of a Bedouin tent rendered in reinforced concrete and granite. One of the largest mosques in the world; a sharp-edged rejection of dome-and-minaret convention.
1988
Americas
Spirit of Haida Gwaii
by Bill Reid
Haida · Bill Reid · 1986–1991
A bronze canoe filled with thirteen mythic figures paddling together — Raven, Bear, Eagle, Wolf, and others. Reid worked on it for five years while Parkinson's overtook him.
1993
Islamic World
Hassan II Mosque
Modern Moroccan · 1986–1993 · Casablanca
Built out over the Atlantic on the king's sixtieth birthday. Its minaret is 210 meters — the second-tallest in the world. The hall's retractable roof opens to the sky.
1997
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Wat Rong Khun (White Temple)
by Chalermchai Kositpipat
Contemporary Thai Buddhist · Chalermchai Kositpipat · 1997 onward · Chiang Rai
An entire temple sculpted white, glittering with shards of mirror. The artist intends it to take centuries to complete; hands rise from hell at the entrance.
2000
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Banaue Rice Terraces
Ifugao · 2000+ years old · Philippines
Stone-walled terraces climbing the mountainside. Built without wheeled carts, draft animals, or cement. If unwrapped they'd circle half the globe.
2005
Africa
Red Bird
by Chris Ofili
Contemporary · c. 2000s · United Kingdom / Nigeria
Chris Ofili's paintings layer resin-coated elephant dung onto glittering collage. His work interrogates Black identity and the politics of representation in Britain.
Images via Wikimedia Commons. Click any image to open it and see its source.
